title: membership.add audit row omits the acting admin (actorPrincipalId hardcoded null)
cwe: CWE-778: Insufficient Logging
description:
Granting a principal access to an organization is a security-relevant action, but its audit row records no actor:

```js
await recordAudit({
  organizationId,
  actorPrincipalId: null,        // <-- who granted the membership is lost
  action: "membership.add",
  targetRef: `identity:${identityId}`,
  decision: "allow",
});
```

The `/v1/memberships` route calls `addMembership(identity.id, ctx.organizationId)` (control-routes.ts:388) and never threads the acting admin's principal through, so `actorPrincipalId` is always null. By contrast the parallel `role.assign` audit correctly carries `grantedBy` (provisioning/index.ts:83-90). The result: the immutable audit chain can prove a membership was added and to whom, but not BY whom.
attack_scenario: An org admin adds an attacker identity (or a backdoor account) to the organization via POST /v1/memberships. Later, during incident response, /v1/audit shows a `membership.add` event for that identity with actorPrincipalId = null — investigators cannot attribute the grant to the admin who performed it, frustrating accountability for privilege-grant abuse.
recommendation: Thread the acting principal into `addMembership` (and pass `ctx.principal.id` from the /v1/memberships route) so the audit row records the granting admin, mirroring `assignRole`'s `grantedBy` handling.
exploitability: Requires an authenticated org-admin principal (gated by ensureAllowed("organization.administer", ...) at control-routes.ts:381). Not reachable pre-auth, not cross-tenant, no privilege gain — it is a same-org trusted-operator accountability/audit-completeness gap, weakening incident-response attribution for membership grants (most acute on the re-invite path where no compensating actor-attributed credential.issue row is written).
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) Confirmed exactly as described. addMembership (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:104-118) hardcodes actorPrincipalId: null in recordAudit at line 112. The /v1/memberships route (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:388) calls addMembership(identity.id, ctx.organizationId) without threading the admin, despite ctx.principal.id being available and used one line earlier (line 387) and across all sibling audited routes (258, 283, 311, 340, 354). The parallel assignRole (provisioning/index.ts:80-90) DOES thread grantedBy into actorPrincipalId, proving the omission is an inconsistency, not intent. recordAudit (src/platform/audit/index.ts:96, 105) persists the null verbatim into the hash-chained row, so the chain can prove a membership was granted and to whom (targetRef identity:<id>) but never BY whom. Partial mitigation exists only on the new-member path: issueMemberCredential (line 387) writes an actor-attributed identity.credential.issue row — but it is skipped when the identity already has a passwordHash (re-invite of an account that exists elsewhere, i.e. the backdoor-account scenario), leaving the actorless membership.add as the sole audit trace with zero attribution. Real CWE-778 gap. || (confirmed) The finding is factually accurate and not refuted by any upstream guard. addMembership (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:104) takes no actor argument and hardcodes actorPrincipalId: null in its membership.add audit row (line 112). recordAudit faithfully persists that null into the hash-chained row (src/platform/audit/index.ts:96,105), so it is durable, not backfilled. The /v1/memberships route DOES have the acting admin in scope — it passes ctx.principal.id to issueMemberCredential on the immediately preceding line (control-routes.ts:387) but calls addMembership(identity.id, ctx.organizationId) without it (control-routes.ts:388). The parallel assignRole correctly threads grantedBy → actorPrincipalId (provisioning/index.ts:85), confirming the omission is an inconsistency, not a design intent. I checked for a mitigating recovery path: on the new-identity invite branch, issueMemberCredential writes an identity.credential.issue row carrying actorPrincipalId: ctx.principal.id for the same identity in the same org stream (provisioning/index.ts:135-142), so a first-time invite is attributable via that adjacent row. BUT the route only runs issueMemberCredential when identity.passwordHash is falsy (control-routes.ts:387); for a re-invited or already-existing identity (precisely the reused 'backdoor account' in the attack scenario), no credential.issue row is written and the membership.add row's null actor is the only record — attribution is genuinely lost. The action is gated by ensureAllowed('organization.administer') (control-routes.ts:381), so it is trusted-operator-only and neither pre-auth nor cross-tenant. Real audit-completeness gap (CWE-778), correctly scoped low.

title: Identity-forging dev shim is gated by an insecure-by-default NODE_ENV string check (fail-open if the env var is absent/misspelled in an alternate on-prem deployment)
cwe: CWE-1188: Insecure Default Initialization of Resource
description: The forgeable raw-subject / X-Identity-Subject shim is toggled by `devShimEnabled() { return process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production"; }` (authn/index.ts:63-65). This is fail-OPEN: the shim is enabled for every NODE_ENV value other than the exact literal "production" — including unset/empty, "prod", or "Production". The shipped Fly/Docker artifact bakes `ENV NODE_ENV=production` (Dockerfile:29, fly.toml), which closes it for that path. But an Ericsson on-prem operator running the app via their own orchestration (a systemd unit, a hand-written k8s manifest, bare `npx tsx src/server.ts`, etc.) that omits NODE_ENV silently re-enables a header-based identity-forge primitive (`Authorization: Bearer <subject>` or `X-Identity-Subject: <subject>`). The blast radius is materially reduced by devSubjectAllowed() (authn/index.ts:69-73), which refuses to forge any identity that has a passwordHash or platform-admin grant — and in steady state every member has a password — so the practical forging surface is thin; this is why the severity is low rather than critical.
attack_scenario: Ericsson deploys via their own process manager and does not set NODE_ENV=production (a realistic on-prem variance from the Fly path). The dev shim activates. If any passwordless platIdentity that holds a membership exists (e.g. created by a future code path, an interrupted invite, or a data import that adds an identity without issuing a credential), an unauthenticated request carrying `X-Identity-Subject: <that-subject>` is accepted as that user with no credential, and X-Org-Id selects their org.
recommendation: Make the gate fail-CLOSED: enable the shim only when an explicit opt-in is present (e.g. NODE_ENV === "development" AND a separate ALLOW_DEV_AUTH_SHIM flag), never as the default for any non-"production" value. Log a loud startup warning whenever the shim is live so a misconfigured on-prem boot is obvious. Document the NODE_ENV requirement in an on-prem deployment runbook.
exploitability: Requires two non-default conditions: (1) an on-prem deployment running OUTSIDE the shipped Docker image (Dockerfile:29 / fly.toml both set NODE_ENV=production) that omits NODE_ENV, and (2) a known, already-existing platIdentity that is active, has passwordHash=null, and is not platform-admin (devSubjectAllowed, authn:70-71). No identity meeting (2) ships by default or is produced by the invite flow (issueMemberCredential runs before addMembership, control-routes.ts:387-388), so a cross-tenant forge of a victim-org member is not reachable against current code. At most an unauthenticated attacker who knows an orphaned passwordless subject string authenticates as a no-org identity and self-creates an org. Not pre-auth cross-tenant, not RCE; a narrow operator-misconfig-gated identity-forge with self-service-signup-level impact.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) The cited fail-open gate is real and exact: src/platform/authn/index.ts:64 is `return process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production"`, which enables the forgeable raw-subject (authn:88-93) and X-Identity-Subject (authn:101-106) shims for every NODE_ENV value other than the literal "production" (unset/"prod"/"Production"/"development"). The shipped Docker image bakes ENV NODE_ENV=production (Dockerfile:29) and fly.toml [env] sets it, so the official deploy path is closed; an on-prem boot via custom orchestration that omits NODE_ENV re-enables the shim — an operator-misconfig precondition, not a default. Exploitability is heavily constrained: devSubjectAllowed (authn:69-73) refuses any identity with a passwordHash or platform-admin grant; the only default-shipped identity is the superadmin, which has both (seedSuperuser provisioning:618-634); and the single member-invite path issues a password via issueMemberCredential BEFORE addMembership (control-routes.ts:382-388), so no passwordless-with-membership identity — the prerequisite for the cross-tenant forge in the attack scenario — is produced by any current code path. A forged subject must also already exist in platIdentity (findUnique by subject, authn:89/102), so no fresh identity can be bootstrapped. The strongest concrete attack on a NODE_ENV-omitting on-prem box is impersonating a known orphaned passwordless/no-org identity, yielding at most self-service org creation (identity-only base context, org-scoped routes fail closed at requireTenant). The finding is not a false positive — the insecure default is genuine and accurately scoped — but it is a defense-in-depth/hardening weakness, not a directly weaponizable cross-tenant break. || (refuted) The cited fail-open property is real but is fully neutralized by an upstream guard, so the described attack is not reachable in shipped code. (1) devShimEnabled() at src/platform/authn/index.ts:64 is indeed `NODE_ENV !== "production"` (fail-open), and the shipped artifact closes it via ENV NODE_ENV=production (Dockerfile:29) and fly.toml:11 — so the only way to re-open it is an operator running outside the shipped artifact without NODE_ENV set. (2) Even when the shim is enabled, the forge primitive is gated a SECOND time by devSubjectAllowed() at src/platform/authn/index.ts:69-73, which refuses to forge any identity that has a passwordHash OR a platform-admin grant. (3) The attack requires a *passwordless identity that holds an active org membership*, and no such object exists in the shipped code: every membership-granting path issues a credential first. The invite path POST /v1/memberships (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:387-388) calls issueMemberCredential() — which sets a scrypt passwordHash (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:131-134) — BEFORE addMembership(); and createOrganization() makes the owner the already-authenticated actor (src/platform/provisioning/index.ts:294-296), who in production authenticated with a real session token and thus has a passwordHash. seedSuperuser() always sets a passwordHash and a platform-admin grant (provisioning/index.ts:618-634), doubly disqualified by devSubjectAllowed. (4) Even if a passwordless identity were forged, it has no membership, so resolveTenantContext returns the identity-only `base` context with no organizationId (authn/index.ts:157-161), and X-Org-Id cannot pivot into any tenant because a non-member non-admin selector throws AuthError (authn/index.ts:148-154). Net: with the shim mis-enabled the worst achievable outcome is an org-less context that can do nothing but create a first org — not the cross-tenant impersonation the finding posits. The fail-open gate is a legitimate defense-in-depth/hardening nit (CWE-1188), but there is no concrete exploitable identity-forge path in the current codebase; the finding's own attack scenario explicitly relies on hypothetical "future code path / interrupted invite / data import" states that the shipped code does not produce.

title: No HSTS header; app serves plain HTTP and relies entirely on an external TLS terminator
cwe: CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
description: The onRequest header hook (src/server.ts:47-51) sets CSP, X-Content-Type-Options and Referrer-Policy but does NOT set Strict-Transport-Security, and the app listens on plain HTTP (PORT 3001). HTTPS is enforced only by Fly's `force_https = true` (fly.toml:23), which does not apply to an on-prem deployment behind Ericsson's own proxy and never emits an HSTS header.
attack_scenario: On an on-prem install without a proxy that injects HSTS, a user who first reaches the app over http:// (typed URL, stale bookmark, internal link) is exposed to SSL-strip / downgrade MITM on that connection, during which the bearer token (sent in the Authorization header) and tenant data traverse cleartext. Absent HSTS the browser never remembers to force https for subsequent visits either.
recommendation: Emit `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains` from the same header hook (gated to https), and document that the on-prem reverse proxy must redirect http->https. This keeps the guarantee app-side rather than depending on a Fly-only setting.
exploitability: Not directly exploitable by the app alone — it requires two external preconditions: (a) an on-path / MITM attacker on the victim's network segment, and (b) an on-prem deployment whose reverse proxy does NOT already redirect http→https or inject HSTS (Fly's force_https covers the cloud demo but not on-prem). Given those, the attack is pre-auth-capable: the auth-exempt login shell at "/" can be served over downgraded http, so typed-URL/stale-bookmark first contact lets the attacker strip TLS and capture either submitted login credentials or the Authorization bearer token in cleartext, then replay them for session hijack. It is not cross-tenant or RCE by itself and needs attacker network position plus a hardening gap in the deployment, so it is a defense-in-depth transport-hardening issue rather than a self-contained app vulnerability — low severity, matching the original rating.
verifier_reasoning: (confirmed) Every factual claim in the finding checks out against the code, and there is NO app-side mitigation. (1) The onRequest header hook at src/server.ts:47-51 emits Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options and Referrer-Policy but never Strict-Transport-Security. (2) A full grep across src/ for strict-transport, hsts, upgrade-insecure-requests, x-forwarded-proto, req.protocol, trustProxy returns ZERO matches — the app has no HSTS header, no upgrade-insecure-requests CSP directive, and no http→https redirect logic anywhere. (3) The app binds plain HTTP via app.listen({ port, host }) at src/server.ts:116 (port 3001 from fly.toml [env]) using Fastify's HTTP server — there is no https.createServer / TLS path; the boot log literally prints `http://${host}:${port}` (src/server.ts:117). (4) The ONLY HTTPS guarantee in the repo is Fly's force_https = true at fly.toml:23, which is a Fly-platform-only edge setting; it does not apply to an on-prem Ericsson deployment behind Ericsson's own proxy, and even on Fly it only redirects http→https — it does NOT emit an HSTS header, so browsers never pin https for future visits in either environment. No upstream guard (auth, PDP, NODE_ENV gate, sanitizer) is relevant to a transport-layer header, so none mitigates this. Reachability: the static login shell at "/" is the one auth-EXEMPT GET (fly.toml health-check comment + tenant-plugin deny-by-default), so a victim can be served the login page and POST credentials over a downgraded cleartext connection BEFORE authenticating, and post-auth the Authorization bearer token traverses the same cleartext — enabling classic SSL-strip/downgrade credential and session theft when an on-path attacker is present and the deployment proxy doesn't itself redirect/inject HSTS. The vulnerability and its described exploit are real with no code-side defense. || (refuted) The code facts are accurate: the onRequest hook at src/server.ts:47-51 sets CSP/nosniff/Referrer-Policy but no Strict-Transport-Security, there is no helmet (grep for strict-transport|hsts|helmet returns nothing across src/ and web/), the app listens on plain HTTP and logs http:// at src/server.ts:116-117, and auth uses a bearer token in the Authorization header (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:242-243). However, the claimed exploit is defeated by upstream mitigation and by the proposed fix being a no-op for its own scenario, so it is a false-positive vulnerability (defense-in-depth gap, not an exploitable flaw): (1) The only environment that actually exists/deploys is Fly, where fly.toml:23 force_https = true terminates TLS and redirects http->https at the edge, eliminating any cleartext path for the real deployment. (2) The on-prem case the finding invents EXPLICITLY presupposes a TLS terminator ('behind Ericsson's own proxy') — and a TLS-terminating reverse proxy is exactly where http->https redirect and HSTS belong; the app correctly listens on plain HTTP behind that terminator (the standard enterprise topology, you do not double-TLS). Missing app-level HSTS therefore requires an ADDITIONAL, independent operator misconfiguration (a TLS proxy with no redirect) PLUS an active on-path MITM to do any harm — it is not independently exploitable. (3) The proposed fix does not even address the stated attack: the attack scenario is a user reaching the app over http://, but per RFC 6797 sec 8.1 browsers MUST ignore an HSTS header delivered over a non-secure (http) response, and the recommendation itself concedes it must be 'gated to https'. So an app that is reached over cleartext (the precondition) can never usefully emit HSTS — the only place HSTS works is on the https responses produced by the very TLS terminator that would also do the redirect. The header is thus a hardening item owned by the reverse-proxy/deploy layer, not an application vulnerability.

title: blobPath() does not validate the hash is hex — a tampered manifest's workflowHash/overlayHash yields path traversal / arbitrary file read into the model loader
cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
description: blobPath() builds `join(ORGS_ROOT, organizationId, "blobs", hash.slice(0, 2), hash)` with no validation that `hash` is a 64-char lowercase-hex string. getVersionContent() (ontology-store.ts:198-201) parses workflowHash/overlayHash out of the manifest *blob body* (not the DB) and passes them straight to cas.get → blobPath. VERIFIED: blobPath(org, "../../../../../../etc/passwd") resolves via node:path.join to `../../../etc/passwd` (the `..` segments collapse out of the org blob dir); blobPath(org, "/etc/passwd") -> `.../blobs/e/etc/passwd`. Because the read path never validates the key (finding above), a tampered manifest can name an arbitrary filesystem path as workflowHash; get() then reads that file and returns it as the 'workflow' model — which loadOntologyFromStrings parses and which GET /v1/ontologies/:id/content (control-routes.ts:494) returns verbatim to the caller, i.e. an arbitrary-file-read primitive. This is chained off the same shared-volume-write precondition as the primary finding (no HTTP route lets a client supply a raw hash to cas.get today; createVersion always recomputes hashes via cas.put), so it is a latent defense-in-depth gap rather than an independently remotely-reachable bug.
attack_scenario: An attacker who can write one blob file on the shared volume rewrites a manifest blob to `{"workflowHash":"../../../../../../etc/passwd","overlayHash":null,"summary":{...}}` (the manifest is itself an unverified CAS blob). On the next read, getVersionContent JSON.parses it and calls cas.get(org, "../../../../../../etc/passwd"); blobPath collapses the traversal to a path outside the org's blob dir, readFileSync returns /etc/passwd (or DATABASE_URL/.env/PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY material), which is loaded as the model and echoed back through GET /v1/ontologies/:id/content — turning a single blob write into arbitrary file disclosure over the API.
recommendation: Validate keys at the trust boundary: in blobPath (or at the top of get/has/put) reject any hash that does not match /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/ before constructing the path. Additionally validate manifest.workflowHash/overlayHash against the same regex in getVersionContent before calling cas.get. Combined with the sha256 self-check from the primary finding, this closes both the corruption and the traversal vectors.
exploitability: No independent exploitability. Requires (1) an authenticated tenant session with PDP ontology.read on the target resource AND (2) the ability to overwrite a specific content-addressed manifest blob file on the host's shared .qlerify/orgs/<org>/blobs volume. No HTTP route can supply a raw or non-hex hash to cas.get (createVersion always recomputes hashes via sha256 hex), so the gap is not remotely reachable, not pre-auth, and not cross-tenant. The sole trigger — arbitrary local filesystem write — already grants a strictly greater capability (direct read of /etc/passwd, .env, PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY, or the DB) than the arbitrary-file-read primitive it produces, so it confers no escalation. Pure defense-in-depth hardening for an on-prem Ericsson install (trusted-operator / already-compromised-host only).
verifier_reasoning:
(confirmed) The code defect is real and exactly as described. blobPath() (src/platform/ontology-store/content-store.ts:28-30) interpolates `hash` into a filesystem path with no hex validation; cas.get/has/put (lines 51-57) add none; and getVersionContent() (src/platform/ontology-store/ontology-store.ts:198-201) JSON.parses the manifest BLOB BODY and passes manifest.workflowHash/overlayHash to cas.get unvalidated. I empirically reproduced the traversal: blobPath(org,"../../../../../../etc/passwd") -> "../../../etc/passwd", escaping the org blob dir; the bytes are then echoed verbatim by GET /v1/ontologies/:id/content (src/platform/http/control-routes.ts:494-496) and GET /v1/workflow/model/content (774-776). HOWEVER, the trigger is NOT API-reachable: v.manifestHash comes only from the DB, written exclusively by createVersion as cas.put(canonicalize({workflowHash, overlayHash, summary})) (ontology-store.ts:127-131), and both inner hashes are sha256(...).digest('hex') (src/platform/ids.ts:27-28) — always 64-char hex. No HTTP request can make a referenced manifest body contain a non-hex workflowHash (a user-supplied body that mimics a malicious manifest is stored at blobs/<its-own-sha256>/ and is never referenced as any version's manifestHash; the canonical manifest shape forces workflowHash to be 64-hex, which cannot also be a traversal string). The only way to inject the traversal is to directly overwrite a manifest blob file on the shared .qlerify/orgs/<org>/blobs volume — i.e. the attacker already holds arbitrary host filesystem write, a capability that strictly exceeds the arbitrary-read primitive this yields (they could read /etc/passwd, .env, PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY, or the SQLite DB directly, or plant a malicious model). The route is also behind requireTenant() + PDP ontology.read (control-routes.ts:489,493), so it is neither pre-auth nor cross-tenant. The finding is accurate AND honestly self-scoped as a latent defense-in-depth gap; the recommended /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/ check at the CAS boundary and in getVersionContent is a valid, cheap hardening, but there is no standalone exploit. || (refuted) The traversal mechanics are real (blobPath at content-store.ts:28-30 does no hex validation; node:path.join collapses `..`; cas.get at content-store.ts:51-54 has no self-check), but the only attacker-controllable hash is impossible to inject through any API path, so the "arbitrary-file-read primitive over the API" is unreachable.

Data-flow trace of the only injection point:
1. getVersionContent (ontology-store.ts:191-199) reads `v.manifestHash` from the DB row platOntologyVersion (select at :193), NOT from any client input, then cas.get(org, v.manifestHash) → JSON.parse → cas.get(org, manifest.workflowHash). The traversal hash can only live inside the manifest BLOB BODY.
2. That manifestHash is server-computed: createVersion (ontology-store.ts:130-131) builds `manifestBytes = canonicalize({workflowHash, overlayHash, summary})` and stores `manifestHash = cas.put(org, manifestBytes)` = sha256 of the canonical manifest. The workflowHash/overlayHash inside it are themselves recomputed server-side via cas.put (ontology-store.ts:127-128). No HTTP route ever lets a client supply a raw hash to cas.get or place a chosen hash into the manifest.
3. Every reader is auth + PDP + org gated: GET /v1/ontologies/:id/content (control-routes.ts:487-496) calls requireTenant() (:489), getOntologyById(org, id) (:491, org-scoped), and ensureAllowed("ontology.read", ...) (:493) before currentContent → getVersionContent. The restore route (control-routes.ts:785-800) additionally requires ensureAllowed("organization.administer"). ensureWorkflowModelLoaded (ontology-store.ts:237-246) is a server-internal onRequest hook.

Therefore the ONLY way to make manifest.workflowHash = "../../../../../../etc/passwd" is to directly overwrite the manifest blob file at .qlerify/orgs/<org>/blobs/<hh>/<manifestHash> on the shared volume — local filesystem write, outside the API/network trust boundary. The finding itself concedes this ("no HTTP route lets a client supply a raw hash to cas.get today ... latent defense-in-depth gap rather than an independently remotely-reachable bug"). An actor with that FS-write capability already (a) can read /etc/passwd, .env, DATABASE_URL, PLATFORM_ENCRYPTION_KEY directly via the same volume access, and (b) can overwrite the workflow body blob with arbitrary model content directly (cas.get has no self-check), so the traversal adds essentially no marginal capability. Adding /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/ validation is still good hygiene/defense-in-depth, but there is no exploitable path that does not already presuppose host/volume compromise.
